It can be directly integrated with strong PTQ baselines such as QuaRot, SpinQuant, and FlatQuant. Experiments on LLaMA-2 and LLaMA-3 show that DynamicPTQ consistently improves perplexity and zero-shot QA performance under W4A4KV4 quantization, while achieving 1.05 to 1.07 times throughput improvement with modest memory overhead. These results demonstrate a practical path toward robust low-bit LLM inference.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces DynamicPTQ, a dynamic quantization technique for improving large language model (LLM) performance under 4-bit quantization. It enhances performance by adapting precision to mitigate dynamic residual challenges.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the challenge of activation quantization in large language model inference under extreme low-bit precision (4-bit), which causes significant quantization errors due to massive activations.
AI.method: The authors identify dynamic instabilities in quantization arising from phase-wise activation changes across layers. They propose DynamicPTQ, which identifies layers sensitive to quantization and assigns higher precision (8-bit) to these layers, while maintaining 4-bit precision elsewhere.
AI.result: DynamicPTQ achieves better perplexity and zero-shot QA performance on LLaMA-2 and LLaMA-3 under W4A4KV4 quantization. It also improves throughput by up to 1.07 times with minimal memory overhead.
AI.conclusion: DynamicPTQ directly addresses the challenges of cross-layer residual dynamics in low-bit quantization and offers an efficient and practical approach for robust LLM inference.

title: MP3: Multi-Period Pattern Pre-training forSpatio-Temporal Forecasting
summary: Spatio-Temporal forecasting is crucial in diverse fields, such as transportation, climate, and energy. Urban spatio-temporal data exhibits temporal mirage: similar short-window inputs have divergent future trends, and vice versa. Existing spatio-temporal graph neural networks (STGNNs) cannot effectively identify such mirages. We argue that the core reason lies in the short-window inputs that have incomplete period observation, heterogeneous global spatial correlation, and cross-period superposition causality. To bridge this gap, we develop a novel Multi- Period Pattern Pre-training (MP3), a plug-and-play pre-training plugin for distinguishing temporal mirages. MP3 presents two core innovations: (1) The multi-period pattern learning is designed to learn multi-period patterns from long time series. Specifically, multi-period temporal modeling leverages edge convolution to identify different multi-period patterns. Multi-period spatial modeling uses a bottleneck project and a global memory bank to capture heterogeneous global spatial relations efficiently. Cross-period pattern interaction employs a causality-enhanced Transformer to capture dependencies across different period patterns. (2) This plugin can seamlessly integrate into existing STGNN backbones to strengthen their forecasting performance. The experiment on five STGNN baselines across five real-world datasets (including a large-scale dataset CA) verify the effectiveness, superior scalability and strong adaptability of MP3, which brings consistent and robust performance improvements across all evaluated baselines. On average, MP3 reduces the MAE 4.7% and the RMSE 5.0%. The code can be available at https://github.com/YAN-outlook/MP3.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MP3, a pre-training plugin designed to address temporal mirages in urban spatio-temporal forecasting and improve accuracy when integrated into existing STGNN models.
AI.motivation: To overcome the challenge of "temporal mirages" in urban spatio-temporal data, where similar short-window input patterns lead to divergent future trends, due to incomplete period observation, heterogeneous spatial correlations, and cross-period causal relationships.
AI.method: The MP3 plugin incorporates multi-period pattern learning with three key modules: edge convolution for temporal patterns, bottleneck projects and memory banks for spatial relationships, and causality-enhanced Transformers for cross-period interaction. MP3 is a flexible addition to existing STGNNs.
AI.result: MP3 demonstrated significant performance improvements when tested on five STGNN baselines using five real-world datasets, including a large-scale CA dataset. It reduced MAE by 4.7% and RMSE by 5.0% on average.
AI.conclusion: The MP3 plugin enhances the forecasting accuracy and robustness of existing STGNNs. Its scalability and adaptability make it suitable for diverse urban spatio-temporal datasets, addressing limitations of traditional models faced with temporal mirages.

title: Skiplists with Foresight: Skipping Cache Misses
summary: A skiplist is a fundamental data structure widely used in systems and applications for indexing data stores. In this work, we introduce Foresight, a cache-friendly skiplist optimization. Extending Foresight to concurrent settings introduces significant synchronization challenges that we identify and address. Foresight is a surgical optimization, easy to integrate into a wide variety of skiplist designs. We apply it to one sequential and three concurrent skiplist designs and observe throughput improvements of up to 45% in microbenchmarks. When applied to a skiplist-based index in the DBx1000 in-memory database, Foresight yields end-to-end performance gains of up to 15%.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Foresight, an optimization for skiplists that improves cache performance and offers significant speed gains.
AI.motivation: To enhance the efficiency and performance of skiplists, a commonly used data structure in indexing systems and applications.
AI.method: Developed a cache-friendly skiplist optimization, Foresight, and extended it to both sequential and concurrent skiplist designs, addressing synchronization challenges.
AI.result: Throughput improvements of up to 45% in microbenchmarks and performance gains of up to 15% in skiplist-based database indexing systems.
AI.conclusion: Foresight is an effective, implementable optimization that improves performance across various skiplist designs, including those in database systems.

title: Understanding the Rejection of Fixes Generated by Agentic Pull Requests -- Insights from the AIDev Dataset
comment: 5 pages, 2 figures, MSR '26: Proceedings of the 23rd International Conference on Mining Software Repositories, April 2026, Rio de Janeiro, Brazil
summary: AI coding agents are increasingly used to generate pull requests (PRs) that propose code fixes in software projects. From a first exploration of the AIDev dataset, we find that 46.41\% of the fixes proposed by the agents Copilot, Devin, Cursor, and Claude are rejected. This represents a significant amount of wasted resources that require human reviews, verifications, and running tests and validations for fixes that are merely discarded. Our goal in this paper is to understand the failure modes of AI-agents, an understanding that is crucial for better integrating AI-agents as efficient teammates. In this paper, we conduct a qualitative study on a representative sample of 306 non-merged pull requests created or co-authored by the agents mentioned earlier, followed by a quantitative analysis of the reasons for rejection. Our qualitative findings identify 14 reasons divided into four high-level categories for rejecting AI-agent fixes. We observe that developers can reject fixes due to fixes whose implementation is incorrect (e.g., incomplete, wrong approach), fixes that do not pass the continuous integration (CI) pipelines and fail tests, fixes for which the agent is unable to perform the implementation (e.g., no code generated, sessions lost), and fixes whose priority is low. Our results shed light on the importance of better guiding the model at these levels: (1) proposing hints about the approach to follow for fixing an issue, (2) outlining constraints or limitations regarding the approaches that should not be taken, and (3) instructing the agent on how to validate the implementation through CI pipelines and without introducing a breaking change. Our results suggest the need for good prioritization of tasks so that generated fixes do not lead to wasted human review efforts or wasted agent resources (e.g., tokens, compute, or allowed number of requests).

AI.tldr: AI coding agents have a high rejection rate of 46.41% for the fixes they propose. The study analyzes why this happens, identifying 14 reasons across four categories. Suggestions include improving AI-guided approaches, constraints, validation processes, and prioritization to minimize wasted resources.
AI.motivation: The main motivation is to understand why a significant portion of AI-generated code fixes are rejected and how this inefficiency can be mitigated to improve AI agents as valuable collaborators in software development.
AI.method: The authors conducted a qualitative analysis on a sample of 306 non-merged pull requests generated or co-authored by AI coding agents, identifying reasons for rejection. This was followed by a quantitative evaluation into the categories of rejections.
AI.result: The study categorizes rejections into four main areas: incorrect implementation (e.g., incomplete, wrong approach), failure to pass CI pipelines and tests, inability to perform the task (e.g., no code generated), and low-priority fixes. This highlights key failure modes of the AI agents.
AI.conclusion: The findings emphasize the need for better guidance for AI agents, including providing hints on fixing approaches, constraints for what not to do, and validation techniques. This could reduce wasted human effort and computational resources while improving the efficiency of AI-agent contributions.

title: From AGI to ASI
summary: Over the last decade, building human-level artificial general intelligence has moved from far-fetched speculation to being a concrete next-decade target for many of the largest AI organisations. Achieving this goal would have profound and far-reaching impacts on human society, which raises many complex questions for the decade ahead. This report investigates how AI itself might continue to develop in a post-AGI world along the continuum of machine intelligence. The endpoint of this continuum, Universal AI, is theoretically well understood, which provides some formal grounding for the main focus of this report: the transition from human-level AGI to artificial general superintelligence, which, intuitively, can be understood as a system that is more intelligent and cognitively capable than large organisations of humans. After characterizing ASI, the report discusses four potential pathways from AGI to ASI: scaling AGI, AI paradigm shifts, recursive improvement, and ASI emerging from large-scale multi-agent collectives. The report then discusses possible frictions and bottlenecks along these pathways. Determining whether the impact of these frictions will be negligible or substantial raises a number of concrete open research questions. Due to large uncertainties for predicting ASI progress, it cannot be ruled out that AI progress might continue to accelerate over the next years. This could imply that the image of a single transformative step change, caused by the introduction of human-level AGI into our society, could be inaccurate. More apt might be the prospect of a series of transformative societal changes caused by AI-enabled progress and breakthroughs across many areas of science and technology. Preparing for this prospect requires a massively interdisciplinary endeavour of global scope and interest.

AI.tldr: The paper examines pathways from AGI to artificial superintelligence (ASI) and potential societal transformations due to AI development, urging interdisciplinary preparation.
AI.motivation: To explore how AI development may progress post-AGI and its potentially transformative impact on society, given the prospect of ASI.
AI.method: Discusses pathways to ASI (e.g., scaling AGI, paradigm shifts, recursive improvement) and examines frictions and uncertainties in these transitions.
AI.result: Highlights the unpredictable nature of ASI progress and envisions the likelihood of continuous transformative changes rather than a single AGI-driven disruption.
AI.conclusion: Preparing for the progression of AI towards ASI requires global interdisciplinary collaboration due to immense societal implications and uncertainties.

title: Computationally tractable robust differentially private mean estimation
summary: We develop a new, differentially private mean estimator called the balloon mean. The main features of the balloon mean are that it is computationally tractable and enjoys robustness to outlying observations. It is based on an iterative clipping procedure over expanding Mahalanobis balls, or ``balloons.'' The method satisfies zero-concentrated differential privacy and depends on a small number of interpretable tuning parameters. We provide theoretical guarantees under heavy-tailed and contaminated elliptical models, characterizing its statistical performance and robustness to outliers. Extensive simulations demonstrate that the balloon mean is robust to heavy-tailed and contaminated data, and outperforms existing differentially private mean estimators in contaminated settings.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the balloon mean, a new differentially private mean estimator emphasizing computational tractability and robustness to outliers.
AI.motivation: Current differentially private mean estimators face challenges in fully addressing robustness to heavy tails and contamination in data, requiring improved methods.
AI.method: The balloon mean employs an iterative clipping procedure within expanding Mahalanobis balls (balloons) satisfying zero-concentrated differential privacy, using few tuning parameters.
AI.result: Theoretical guarantees under heavy-tailed and contaminated models ensure robustness; simulations confirm superior performance over existing estimators in contaminated data scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The balloon mean offers a practical, robust, and theoretically-grounded solution for differentially private mean estimation in challenging data settings.

title: SalArt-VQA: Diagnosing Whether VLMs Understand Salient Artifacts in Generated Images
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) are increasingly used to detect whether AI-generated images contain visible artifacts, yet their ability to analyze such artifacts remains poorly understood. A correct image-level decision can still hide important failures: a model may correctly flag an artifact while relying on the wrong visual cue, selecting the wrong region, or describing a defect that the image does not support. To evaluate these behaviors directly, we introduce SalArt-VQA, a diagnostic benchmark for fine-grained SALient ARTifact understanding in AI-generated images. SalArt-VQA contains 950 images and 3,681 human-authored multiple-choice questions spanning artifact images, matched real reference images, and paired generated reference images. Four aligned question types evaluate presence detection, semantic localization, spatial grounding, and evidence-grounded defect identification, while the reference splits test calibration and abstention when the annotated defect is absent. Across 20 VLMs, SalArt-VQA reveals failures that image-level detection accuracy hides: the strongest model reaches 99.37% detection recall on artifact images but answers all four artifact-side questions correctly on only 53.26% of images. Comparing artifact images with artifact-free references reveals a sensitivity-calibration tradeoff: sensitive models often make unsupported artifact claims, while conservative models avoid false alarms largely by missing real artifacts. These results show that high artifact detection accuracy alone does not imply grounded artifact understanding. SalArt-VQA exposes these hidden failure modes and provides a fine-grained evaluation of whether VLM artifact claims are supported by local visual evidence.

AI.tldr: This paper presents SalArt-VQA, a benchmark to evaluate the nuanced understanding of AI-generated image artifacts by vision-language models, showing that high accuracy in artifact detection can mask underlying failures in correctly identifying and explaining artifacts.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to better understand and address the limitations of vision-language models in identifying and analyzing visible artifacts in AI-generated images. High detection accuracy might obscure deeper failures in artifact interpretation, such as incorrect localization or unsupported claims.
AI.method: The study introduces SalArt-VQA, a diagnostic benchmark comprising 950 images and 3,681 human-authored questions designed to evaluate fine-grained artifact understanding. The questions cover detection, semantic localization, spatial grounding, and defect identification, and include reference comparisons to test calibration and abstention when defects are absent.
AI.result: SalArt-VQA reveals significant gaps in the models' artifact understanding: while one model achieves 99.37% detection recall, it answers all artifact-related questions correctly for only 53.26% of images. Sensitivity-calibration tradeoffs are highlighted, with sensitive models making unsupported claims and conservative models missing real artifacts.
AI.conclusion: The results demonstrate that high detection accuracy does not guarantee meaningful artifact understanding. SalArt-VQA introduces a critical tool to reveal hidden failure modes in models, enabling fine-grained evaluation of their ability to detect and interpret visual evidence for artifacts.

title: Does AI Reviewer See the Full Picture? Attacking and Defending Multimodal Peer Review
comment: Accepted to ICML 2026, Project Page: https://paper-guard.github.io/
summary: The integration of Large Language Models (LLMs) and Multimodal LLMs (MLLMs) into scientific peer-review workflows introduces novel and significant risks for adversarial manipulation, especially given the multimodal nature of scientific papers where figures, not just text, convey core evidence. This creates a significant gap: current robustness studies on AI peer-review are overwhelmingly text-only. Moreover, the problem is distinct from standard jailbreaking, as a peer-review attack seeks to induce a domain-specific, targeted failure (e.g., "inflate this score") rather than a general safety policy violation, for which no practical defenses exist. To address this, we introduce PaperGuard, the first comprehensive benchmark designed to systematically evaluate and defend AI-generated peer-review against these domain-specific, cross-modal attacks. Our framework is built on three pillars: (1) a new multimodal peer-review dataset spanning multiple scientific domains; (2) a unified suite of attacks, including black-box prompt injections and white-box perturbations, specifically designed to target both text (GCG) and figures (PGD); and (3) a practical defense, motivated by the long-context challenge of academic papers, that uses chunk-based embedding search to efficiently localize and mitigate harmful instructions. Our extensive experiments, conducted across state-of-the-art models, confirm that AI reviewers are pervasively vulnerable. PaperGuard establishes the foundational benchmark, protocols, and actionable defense necessary to pioneer trustworthy, attack-resilient AI-assisted scholarly reviewing.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces 'PaperGuard,' a framework that addresses the vulnerabilities of AI-assisted peer reviews to targeted manipulative attacks in both text and figures within scientific papers.
AI.motivation: Current AI peer-review systems, particularly those incorporating LLMs and MLLMs, lack robustness against adversarial cross-modal manipulations in scientific workflows, leaving a significant gap in trustworthy scholarly review.
AI.method: The authors propose a benchmark composed of a multimodal peer-review dataset, a suite of targeted attacks for text and figures, and a chunk-based embedding search mechanism for localizing and mitigating harmful instructions.
AI.result: Empirical experiments reveal that AI peer-review systems are broadly susceptible to both text and figure-based attacks. PaperGuard provides a systematic way to evaluate vulnerabilities and offers a practical defense mechanism.
AI.conclusion: PaperGuard establishes essential tools and strategies to advance secure and resilient AI-driven peer-review systems, filling an important gap in academic integrity defenses.

title: EquiDexFlow: Contact-Grounded SE(3)-Equivariant Dexterous Grasp Generative Flows
comment: 22 pages, 11 figures, 11 tables. Project page with videos, code, and checkpoints: https://equidexflow.github.io
summary: Most learned dexterous grasp generators relegate contact forces to a downstream verification step, so a kinematically-plausible pose can still violate the conditions for a stable physical grasp. We address this with EquiDexFlow, an SE(3)-equivariant flow-matching model that jointly predicts wrist pose, joint angles, fingertip contacts, surface normals, and contact forces from an object point cloud. Our architecture projects contacts onto the object surface and forces into the Coulomb friction cone by construction, so placement and friction compliance hold without loss penalties. We prove end-to-end SE(3) equivariance and verify it empirically over 200 rotations, with wrist residuals below $0.04^\circ$ and exactly zero joint deviation. Trained on 8,100 force-closure grasps across 81 objects for the 16-DoF Allegro Hand, our model achieves zero friction violations, the best composite score, and the lowest wrench residual among all ablation variants. We retarget decoded fingertip contacts to a 16-DoF LEAP Hand via per-finger inverse kinematics, and our hardware-feasible refinement places every joint at least 5% inside its actuator envelope while preserving wrench balance. On the physical robot, retargeted EquiDexFlow-decoded grasps complete open-loop pick-and-hold trials on all six test objects, with every asymmetric object succeeding at both the canonical pose and a $120^\circ$ co-rotation. Videos, code, and checkpoints are available at https://equidexflow.github.io.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces EquiDexFlow, a model combining SE(3)-equivariant flow-matching with force-aware dexterous grasp synthesis, achieving state-of-the-art performance in simulations and physical trials.
AI.motivation: Current learned dexterous grasp generators often fail to ensure stable physical grips due to ignoring contact force constraints during generation.
AI.method: The EquiDexFlow model predicts wrist pose, joint angles, fingertip contacts, surface normals, and force compliance from object point clouds, with built-in guarantees for Coulomb friction and SE(3)-equivariance.
AI.result: Trained on 8,100 grasps for the Allegro Hand, the model achieves friction-compliant grasping without violations and performs well on simulated and physical dexterous grasping tasks.
AI.conclusion: EquiDexFlow proves effective in addressing both kinematic and dynamic stability of grasps, demonstrating utility in robotic object manipulation under diverse conditions.

title: A Stationary (and Therefore Compatible) Representation is All You Need
comment: Accepted to TPAMI2026. Extension of the CVPR2024 version (arXiv:2405.02581)
summary: Learning compatible representations aims to learn feature representations that can be used interchangeably over time whenever a model undergoes updates. In this paper, we demonstrate that stationary representations learned by d-Simplex fixed classifiers imply compatibility as in its formal definition. This result establishes a foundation for future works and can be directly exploited in practical learning scenarios. We address the challenge of learning compatibility using $d$-Simplex fixed classifiers when the model is sequentially fine-tuned. Learning according to a d-Simplex fixed classifier with the cross-entropy loss aligns feature distributions at the first-order statistics. Consequently, it may not fully capture higher-order dependencies in the representation between model updates. To address this issue, we demonstrate that training the model using a $d$-Simplex fixed classifier through a convex combination of the cross-entropy loss and a contrastive loss not only captures higher-order dependencies, but is also equivalent to learning with the cross-entropy under the compatibility constraints. We confirm our findings with extensive experiments also considering a new scenario where a pre-trained model is sequentially fine-tuned and occasionally replaced with an improved model. We show that stationary representations enable uninterrupted retrieval services (without reprocessing gallery images) while improving performance during model updates and replacements, achieving state-of-the-art. Code at https://github.com/miccunifi/iamcl2r.

AI.tldr: This paper explores learning compatible representations using $d$-Simplex fixed classifiers to maintain model compatibility during updates, addressing challenges with first-order and higher-order dependencies.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to solve the problem of maintaining interchangeable feature representations over time, even as models undergo updates or replacements, ensuring uninterrupted system functionality.
AI.method: The study proposes using $d$-Simplex fixed classifiers with a convex combination of cross-entropy loss and contrastive loss to learn stationary representations that align feature distributions and capture higher-order dependencies.
AI.result: The paper demonstrates that stationary representations via $d$-Simplex fixed classifiers enable retrieval services without reprocessing and improve performance across model updates. Extensive experiments support the proposed methods, achieving state-of-the-art performance.
AI.conclusion: The findings validate that $d$-Simplex fixed classifiers are effective for learning compatibility, addressing both feature distribution alignment and higher-order dependencies, and laying a foundation for future research.

title: Work Stealing for the 2D-Mesh Topology of Satellite Constellations in Low Earth Orbit
summary:
Asynchronous Many-Task (AMT) is a parallel programming model used in High Performance Computing (HPC). An AMT runtime can distribute fine-grained tasks across processing units called workers, through work stealing: when a worker has no tasks left to process, it tries to steal tasks from other workers. Workers are not restricted to a single compute node but can also be distributed across multiple nodes of an HPC cluster. Existing AMT runtimes assume a fully connected network with low, uniform latency and perform global work stealing, selecting another worker at random from all workers in the system.
  Space Edge Computing (SEC) uses constellations of satellites in Low Earth Orbit (LEO) as distributed compute clusters. Unlike HPC clusters, LEO satellites communicate through inter-satellite links that form a sparse mesh topology. Reaching a distant satellite requires multiple hops, each adding latency.
  As a step toward adapting AMT to SEC, this paper proposes a neighbor-only work stealing strategy in which workers steal exclusively from directly connected neighbors, avoiding multi-hop communication. An analytical model shows that restricting stealing this way yields a per-attempt latency advantage that grows with constellation size. Preliminary experiments on an HPC cluster with an emulated mesh over uniform low-latency links isolate the effect of victim selection: the neighbor-only strategy performs within ~2.2% of global stealing on both balanced and irregular workloads, indicating that restricting the victim set does not harm load balancing in this setting. Taken together, the experiments suggest that neighbor-only stealing can be on a par with global stealing, and the model suggests that neighbor-only stealing becomes preferable at scale.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a neighbor-only work stealing strategy for Space Edge Computing (SEC) to address unique communication constraints, showing it can match and even outperform global stealing as system scale increases.
AI.motivation: To adapt the Asynchronous Many-Task (AMT) programming model, common in High Performance Computing (HPC), for Space Edge Computing (SEC) using Low Earth Orbit (LEO) satellite networks, which have sparse, high-latency mesh topologies unlike typical HPC setups.
AI.method: Develop and evaluate a neighbor-only work stealing strategy where tasks are stolen exclusively from directly connected neighboring workers (satellites) to avoid high-latency multi-hop communication. Analytical models and HPC-based experiments emulate the mesh network effects.
AI.result: The proposed neighbor-only strategy achieves load balancing performance within ~2.2% of global stealing in both uniform and irregular workloads in an emulated HPC mesh environment. Analytical modeling suggests this approach offers latency advantages as the system size increases.
AI.conclusion: Restricting work stealing to neighbors in sparse, high-latency environments, such as satellite networks, can provide comparable performance to global strategies while improving scalability, demonstrating its potential suitability for Space Edge Computing.

title: AI SciBrief as a Gateway to Research: A Framework for Onboarding Students into New Research Areas
comment: This is the version of the article accepted for publication in TELE 2025 after peer review. The final, published version is available at IEEE Xplore: https://doi.org/10.1109/TELE66816.2025.11211989
summary: Students at all levels of higher education face a significant barrier in the form of information overload, which often paralyzes the initial stages of the research process and suppresses motivation. In response, this article introduces a pedagogical framework that leverages AI SciBrief, a platform powered by a Large Language Model (LLM) designed to automatically generate digests of scientific trends. We describe how this multidisciplinary tool - with initial coverage in finance, medicine, and education - can be integrated into the curriculum to overcome this "entry barrier." The framework provides concrete methodologies for utilizing these digests to facilitate topic selection for term papers, accelerate literature reviews for dissertations, and enable postgraduate students to continuously monitor emerging trends. We conclude that AI SciBrief functions as a "gateway to research" effectively reducing students' cognitive load and empowering them to transition more rapidly from information searching to knowledge creation.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces AI SciBrief, an AI-driven platform that helps students overcome information overload by generating digests of scientific trends, which aids in research and knowledge creation.
AI.motivation: Students struggle with information overload, impeding the initial stages of research and reducing their motivation.
AI.method: The authors present a pedagogical framework that integrates AI SciBrief into the curriculum to help students with tasks like topic selection, literature reviews, and trend monitoring.
AI.result: The framework effectively reduces students’ cognitive load by providing automated digests of scientific trends, helping them transition quickly to productive research activities.
AI.conclusion: AI SciBrief serves as a valuable tool in higher education, aiding students to overcome informational barriers and promoting effective research engagement.

title: Deployment-Centered Evaluation: Predicting Query-Level Rejection Risk in a Clinical LLM System
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly integrated into clinical systems, making it essential to evaluate the real-world utility of these systems. However, static benchmarks tend to measure correctness rather than user acceptance, aggregate performance across queries, and require densely annotated datasets -- leading to major blind spots for evaluating clinical systems. In this work, we perform a deployment-centered evaluation of an LLM system embedded within electronic health records at an academic medical center, where user feedback is sparse but closely reflects the deployment conditions. Specifically, we train a pre-response classifier that estimates the risk that a future interaction will result in the user rejecting the LLM response, based on query content and deployment-specific context available before generation. We conduct a prospective analysis of our model over 4.5 months of user feedback, finding that our prediction model achieves an AUROC of 0.719. Further, we estimate the benefit of such predictions in two downstream use cases (guardrail triggering and abstention). Our key conceptual insight is that making use of deployment-specific context (i.e., the provider type, department name, language model used for response), as opposed to only query content, improves the ability to predict whether the user will reject the system output. Altogether, our empirical case study demonstrates the feasibility of predicting user rejection using deployment-specific context, opening the door to targeted guardrails.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates the real-world utility of large language models (LLMs) in clinical systems by predicting user rejection using deployment-specific context in an academic medical center.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address shortcomings in static benchmarks, which prioritize correctness over user acceptance and have blind spots for deployment-specific contexts in evaluating clinical systems.
AI.method: A pre-response classifier was trained to estimate the likelihood of user rejection based on query content and deployment-specific context. The model's performance was analyzed over 4.5 months with real-world user feedback.
AI.result: The prediction model achieved an AUROC of 0.719 and demonstrated that using deployment-specific context improves the ability to predict user rejection. Two downstream use cases (guardrail triggering and abstention) showed additional utility.
AI.conclusion: The study confirms the feasibility of using deployment-specific context to predict user rejection, paving the way for targeted systems like guardrails to improve LLM performance in clinical systems.

title: Physics-Informed Neural Networks for Chemotherapy Pharmacokinetics: Benchmarking the Clinical Estimator and Exposing Parameter Identifiability
summary:
Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) are an attractive tool for partial-observation problems in biology, where the governing dynamics are known but some compartments cannot be measured. Chemotherapy pharmacokinetics (PK) is a clean instance: drug concentration in plasma is routinely measured, but concentration in tissue -- which determines tumour kill and off-target toxicity -- is not. We benchmark a PINN against the standard clinical baseline (nonlinear least-squares on the analytical biexponential plasma solution, hereafter NLS) and a physics-agnostic neural baseline (a data-only MLP) on two PK problems. On the linear two-compartment problem, NLS is near-optimal; the PINN matches it to within a small constant factor while also producing the tissue curve in a single training pass, whereas the data-only MLP fails on tissue by roughly 10x. On a Michaelis-Menten extension (saturable elimination), the biexponential closed form no longer exists, so NLS is mis-specified and silently returns meaningless rate constants. The PINN instead exposes a deeper fact: the Michaelis-Menten two-compartment model is non-identifiable from plasma alone, and the PINN reports this honestly by converging to a basin with k12 -> 0. Adding two sparse tissue observations largely resolves identifiability: across five seeds the PINN recovers k21 to within 1% of truth and Vmax, Km to within one standard-deviation bar, while k12 moves in the correct direction (0.02 -> 0.82) but remains ~2 sigma below truth -- a recovery the closed-form NLS estimator cannot attempt at all, because its biexponential ansatz describes only plasma. Our claim is not that PINNs beat NLS. It is that PINNs offer a uniform recipe that ties the textbook estimator on the textbook problem, exposes structural identifiability that
  the textbook estimator hides, and absorbs heterogeneous measurements within a single loss.

AI.tldr: The paper examines the benefits of Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) over traditional methods and physics-agnostic models in modeling chemotherapy pharmacokinetics (PK), showing that PINNs provide better insights and handle complex scenarios more effectively.
AI.motivation: To explore how Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) can address challenges in partially observed biological systems, particularly in chemotherapy pharmacokinetics where tissue drug concentration remains unmeasured.
AI.method: The study benchmarks PINNs against standard nonlinear least squares (NLS) and a data-only multilayer perceptron (MLP) in two pharmacokinetics scenarios: a linear two-compartment model and a Michaelis-Menten saturable elimination model.
AI.result: In the first scenario, PINNs and NLS achieved similar outcomes, but PINNs additionally inferred tissue concentration. In the second scenario, PINNs highlighted models' non-identifiability, improved parameter recovery with sparse tissue observations, and surpassed NLS when analytical solutions were unavailable.
AI.conclusion: PINNs provide a consistent framework that matches traditional methods in simpler cases and excels in uncovering model weaknesses or addressing complex and heterogeneous data problems.

title: VLADriveBench: Evaluating CoT-Action Relationship in VLA for Autonomous Driving
summary: Vision-language-action (VLA) models generate chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning alongside driving trajectories, but existing benchmarks evaluate only trajectory quality and do not assess whether the CoT is relevant, consistent, or causally connected to the driving action. We introduce VLADriveBench, a framework that combines observational metrics (mentioning, hallucination, contradiction, action alignment) with a CoT intervention protocol to provide complementary views of the CoT-action relationship. Applying VLADriveBench to three models across two architectures, we find that the two analyses can diverge sharply: ORION scores highest on observational alignment yet its CoT is epiphenomenal, while Alpamayo v1.5 scores lower yet its CoT is strongly causal, with visual salience gating the extent of CoT influence.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces VLADriveBench, a benchmark for evaluating the coherence of chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning and driving actions in Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, highlighting major differences in CoT-action alignment across models.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks for VLA models fail to assess the causal and coherent relationship between chain-of-thought reasoning and driving actions, which limits the comprehensive evaluation of such systems.
AI.method: VLADriveBench combines observational metrics (mentioning, hallucination, contradiction, action alignment) and a CoT intervention protocol to evaluate the relevance, consistency, and causality of CoT in driving tasks.
AI.result: Applying VLADriveBench to three models, the results show divergence in performance: ORION excels in observational alignment but has epiphenomenal CoT, whereas Alpamayo v1.5 has lower alignment scores but exhibits causally influential CoT.
AI.conclusion: VLADriveBench provides a nuanced evaluation framework that exposes differences in how VLA models integrate CoT reasoning and driving actions, underscoring the need to assess causal connections beyond trajectory quality.

title: Agent-based models for the evolution of morphological alternation patterns
summary:
Why is the past of English "go" the apparently unrelated "went"? Such alternations are frequent in languages. They neither aid communication nor learnability, yet they can be persistent, surviving over centuries or millennia.
  We present a multi-agent simulation of the emergence of morphological stem and inflection alternations. Alternate forms arise by phonological changes or, as with "go/went", from lexical alternatives associated with a subset of the population. When an agent 'hears' another agent use a novel form for a slot in the paradigm of a word (say, the past tense of go), they will with some probability adopt that form, possibly spreading its use to other slots in the paradigm that shared the same original form. Thus alternative forms can spread through the population and become entrenched as stem or inflectional marker alternants. Unlike many previous computational studies, our system allows for naturalistic lexical forms, realistic phonological rules, lexicons with hundreds or thousands of entries, and agent populations in the tens or hundreds. It supports several network topologies, diffusion patterns and agent adoption policies.
  One issue with such simulations is evaluation: how realistic is the resulting morphology compared to those of real languages? We introduce the AI Historical Linguist, a novel Large Language Model-driven system that models a debate between two historical linguists. We use this to compare a set of real language morphologies, disguised morphologies, and experimentally evolved morphologies. The results suggest that among the factors that favor more plausible morphologies are scale-free social networks and random Bernoulli adoption of forms.
  We also present three case studies modeling attested historical changes, allowing us to test what might have happened if history had been different.
  All code and data are released.

AI.tldr: The paper explores how irregular morphological forms in languages, like "go/went," emerge and persist through computational multi-agent simulations and introduces a novel evaluation method leveraging a Large Language Model (dubbed the AI Historical Linguist).
AI.motivation: The study aims to understand why irregular morphological alternations persist in languages despite offering no obvious advantages for communication or learnability, addressing a gap in understanding their origins and entrenchment over time.
AI.method: The authors run multi-agent simulations, where agents adopt and spread alternate morphological forms via phonological changes or lexical alternatives. They model real-world linguistic elements like lexicons, phonological rules, and social network structures while varying network topologies and adoption policies.
AI.result: The experiments suggest that factors such as scale-free social networks and random Bernoulli adoption policies result in more plausible morphologies. Additionally, case studies mimic historical shifts under alternative scenarios, testing hypotheses about changes in language morphology.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that realistic morphological alternations can emerge and persist under specific social and adoption network conditions. The incorporation of an AI Historical Linguist provides a novel way to validate morphologies against real-world languages. The findings and newly developed tools hold potential for advancing historical linguistics computationally.

title: Sparse2Act: Learning Action-Aligned Sparse 3D Representations for Cross-Domain Robot Manipulation
summary: Explicit 3D representations are attractive for manipulation because they expose object shape, workspace geometry, and robot-object relations in metric coordinates. However, sparse 3D encoders are often learned through downstream task objectives, tying the representation to a particular data distribution, policy architecture, and action parameterization. We introduce Sparse2Act, an observation-action alignment framework for pretraining sparse point-cloud encoders. The key idea is to use task-space end-effector actions as geometric supervision: masked sparse 3D tokens are trained to organize scene features around the workspace motion paired with the observation. After pretraining, only the encoder initialization is reused by downstream policies, allowing them to retain their own architectures and action spaces, including joint-space commands. On the LIBERO-10 benchmark, our method achieves 86.9% average success after 500 fine-tuning steps. The same pretrained encoder supports LIBERO-to-Meta-World cross-domain transfer, achieving 73.4% average success on the Meta-World-5 benchmark. Ablations on the objective and decoder capacity show that the gains come from the masked action-alignment signal and remain useful across downstream action decoders. In real-world experiments, simulation pretraining followed by limited real-data fine-tuning achieves an average success rate of 72.5% across four tasks, demonstrating effective sim-to-real transfer. These results suggest that robot actions can provide compact geometric supervision for reusable sparse 3D representations.

AI.tldr: Sparse2Act is a framework for pretraining sparse point-cloud encoders using robot end-effector actions for geometric supervision, achieving high success rates across benchmarks and sim-to-real transfer tasks.
AI.motivation: To overcome the challenges of tying 3D representations to specific policies, data distributions, and action spaces, while enabling reusable and generalizable sparse 3D encoders.
AI.method: Sparse2Act leverages task-space end-effector actions as geometric supervision by pretraining masked sparse 3D tokens to organize scene features around workspace motion paired with observations.
AI.result: The framework achieved 86.9% success on LIBERO-10 after fine-tuning, 73.4% success in LIBERO-to-Meta-World transfer, and 72.5% average success in real-world sim-to-real experiments.
AI.conclusion: Sparse2Act demonstrates that robot actions can effectively serve as compact geometric supervision, enabling reusable sparse 3D representations applicable across different tasks and domains.

title: Robustness Verification of Recurrent Neural Networks with Abstraction Refinement
summary: Certified local robustness verification for recurrent neural networks (RNNs) is challenging because approximation errors introduced by nonlinear relaxations can propagate through recurrent connections and accumulate over time. As a result, scalable linear bound propagation methods often become overly conservative and fail to certify inputs that are in fact robust, especially when many pre-activation intervals cross zero. We propose an abstraction-refinement framework for RNN verification that partitions such intervals to remove the dominant relaxation error: on each refined branch, ReLU becomes exact, and smooth activations such as tanh and sigmoid admit substantially tighter linear envelopes. To control the combinatorial cost of splitting in long sequences, we introduce a SHAP-guided timestep selection strategy that ranks hidden states by their contribution to the verification objective and refines only the most critical timesteps in temporal order. Experiments on CIFAR10 and MNIST stroke benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements in verification success and robustness-margin tightness over abstraction-only baselines, while exposing clear runtime trade-offs between ReLU and tanh models.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a method for verifying recurrent neural networks' local robustness by reducing accumulated relaxation errors using abstraction-refinement and prioritizing critical timesteps.
AI.motivation: Existing certified robustness verification for RNNs often fails due to accumulated errors from nonlinear relaxations, especially when dealing with multiple pre-activation intervals crossing zero.
AI.method: The authors propose an abstraction-refinement approach that partitions pre-activation intervals to reduce relaxation error. They also introduce a SHAP-guided timestep selection strategy to refine only the most influential timesteps for scalability.
AI.result: Experimental results on CIFAR10 and MNIST datasets show improved verification success rates and tighter robustness margins compared to benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Combining abstraction-refinement with SHAP-guided timestep selection improves robustness verification for RNNs while balancing computational costs, unveiling trade-offs between ReLU and tanh models.

title: Temporal Conductance and Bounds on the Voter Model for Dynamic Networks
summary:
The voter model is a classical stochastic process that models how opinions might spread through a network: at each step, every node lazily adopts the opinion of a random neighbour; eventually all nodes share the same opinion (consensus). Stronger connectivity should yield faster consensus. Berenbrink, Giakkoupis, Kermarrec, and Mallmann-Trenn (ICALP 2016) make this precise via the network's conductance: if the network has $m$ edges, minimum degree $d_{\min}$, and conductance at least $φ$, then the voter model reaches consensus in expected $O(m/(d_{\min}φ))$ steps. Their results extend to dynamic networks with fixed vertex degrees by considering the network's conductance at each time step.
  We introduce temporal conductance $Φ$, a more general connectivity measure for dynamic networks. Unlike static conductance, which collapses to $0$ whenever some snapshot is disconnected, $Φ$ captures connectivity through edges that appear at different times. We generalise the results of Berenbrink et al. from static conductance to temporal conductance, showing that the expected consensus time of the standard voter model is at most $O(m/(d_{\min}Φ))$. Moreover, we prove that this bound is tight up to constant factors. We expect temporal conductance to be a useful primitive for analysing other dynamics on temporal networks, and potentially time-inhomogeneous Markov chains more generally.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces temporal conductance ($Φ$) as a measure for connectivity in dynamic networks, extending results on the voter model from static to dynamic network contexts.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the limitations of static conductance in dynamic networks where connectivity can evolve over time, and provide a refined metric (temporal conductance) to model and analyze such systems more accurately.
AI.method: The authors generalize prior results on the voter model's consensus time to incorporate temporal conductance, enabling the analysis of how opinions spread in dynamic networks over time.
AI.result: The expected consensus time of the voter model is shown to be $O(m/(d_{\min}Φ))$, where $Φ$ is the temporal conductance. This bound is proven to be tight up to constant factors.
AI.conclusion: Temporal conductance offers a robust tool to analyze consensus time and could serve as a foundation for studying other dynamics on temporal networks or time-inhomogeneous Markov chains.

title: Knowing the Rules Is Not Enough: Student Regulatory Awareness and Use of GenAI in Higher Education
summary: Context: Generative Artificial Intelligence (GenAI) tools such as ChatGPT are increasingly integrated into students learning practices. While previous research mainly examines adoption rates and attitudes, students awareness of institutional regulations and their perceived compliance remain unexplored. Understanding whether regulatory awareness influences student behavior is therefore important as higher education institutions create and apply AI policies. Objective: This study investigates how students awareness of GenAI regulations relates to their perceived compliance and actual usage behavior. Our research objective is to examine the association between regulatory knowledge, GenAI use, and perceived rule conformity among students in computer science related study programs. Method: A survey with 151 undergraduate students in Business Information Systems and E-Government programs at the University of Applied Sciences and Arts Hannover (Germany) collected data on GenAI usage, tools used, awareness of institutional regulations, and perceived compliance. Descriptive statistics, cross-tabulations, and correlation analyzes were applied. Results: Most students actively use GenAI tools, but over half are uncertain whether their usage complies with institutional regulations. Regulatory awareness shows only weak to moderate associations with actual usage behavior. Students primarily rely on privately accessed GenAI tools rather than institutionally provided solutions. Contributions: The study contributes empirical evidence on the relationship between regulatory awareness and GenAI usage in higher education. Our findings highlight a gap between institutional regulations and student practices and provide insights for educators and institutions on improving policy communication and integrating GenAI more effectively into teaching and learning contexts.

AI.tldr: This study explores how students' awareness of Generative AI (GenAI) regulations affects their behavior and compliance, revealing a weak link between regulatory knowledge and usage while highlighting gaps in institutional policy communication.
AI.motivation: The paper is motivated by the increasing integration of GenAI tools like ChatGPT in education, highlighting the need to investigate students' awareness of institutional policies and their impact on behavior, given the lack of previous research in this specific area.
AI.method: A survey of 151 undergraduate students in Germany was conducted, examining their GenAI usage, awareness of regulations, and perceived compliance. The data was analyzed using descriptive statistics, cross-tabulations, and correlations.
AI.result: Most students actively use GenAI tools, but over half are unsure if they comply with institutional regulations. Regulatory awareness shows weak to moderate associations with actual usage, and students prefer private tools over institutional ones.
AI.conclusion: There is a disconnect between institutional regulations and student practices regarding GenAI, suggesting that better policy communication and integration of AI tools into teaching could improve compliance and educational alignment.

title: Definitional alignment before capability alignment: a Design-Science framework for adjudicating claims about AGI
summary: Claims that artificial general intelligence has already arrived and claims that it remains decades away are often defended from overlapping evidence. "AGI" lacks a single shared and stable referent and competing operationalizations can return different verdicts on the same system. This article treats that under-specification as a design and governance problem. Following Design Science Research Methodology, it develops DAF-AGI, a second-order conceptual artifact with two coupled components: five ordinal criteria for assessing the adjudicative fitness of candidate definitions and a structured governance audit of authorship, interest, certification, external verification and revision authority. The artifact is demonstrated on five prominent measurement families and one deflationary boundary position in a documented corpus and then stress-tested against a stylized strong arrival claim: that current generative systems constitute AGI because they outperform a well-educated adult on many cognitive tasks. On evidence from the cited 2024-2025 sources, the claim was certifiable only under a performance-based operationalization; capability-ontology, psychometric and skill-acquisition approaches did not certify it, the economic family remains indeterminate and the deflationary position refuses binary adjudication. The contribution is a novel integration and operationalization, not an empirical validation: independent application, inter-rater testing and author-external cases remain necessary. The paper further proposes definitional sovereignty as an enabling component of algorithmic sovereignty: the institutional capacity to contest, certify and revise imported technological categories under public accountability.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a structured framework for assessing and governing definitions of artificial general intelligence (AGI), treating its ambiguous and contested definitions as a problem of governance and decision-making.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the under-specification and competing definitions of AGI, which lead to conflicting evaluations of whether current AI systems qualify as AGI. This lack of clarity constitutes a governance and design problem.
AI.method: The authors propose DAF-AGI, a conceptual framework with ordinal criteria for evaluating definitions and a governance audit. They apply this framework to test AGI claims against multiple definitional families and stress-test it using a specific claim about generative systems.
AI.result: When applied to a stylized AGI arrival claim, DAF-AGI results showed certification only under performance-based definitions, with other approaches either failing to certify the claim or remaining inconclusive. The contribution remains a conceptual framework rather than empirical validation.
AI.conclusion: DAF-AGI offers a structured approach to contest and govern AGI definitions. The work emphasizes the need for institutional capacity to revise and challenge technological classifications under public accountability, termed as 'definitional sovereignty.'

title: How Useful is Causal Invariance for Domain Adaptation in Finite-Sample Settings?
summary:
Machine learning models often degrade when they are deployed on a target distribution that differs from the source distributions they were trained on. Recent work in causality-based domain generalization has shown how shared causal structure between domains can induce invariant predictors, e.g., models on a subset of features which have stable risk across structured domain shifts. However, the extent to which such population-level causal invariances can lead to gains in finite-sample settings remains underexplored. In particular, in practice we often have access to a few labeled target samples, a setting called supervised domain adaptation (sDA). In this paper, we explore when (full or partial) causal knowledge can provably improve supervised domain adaptation.
  As a first step, we study linear regression, where full or partial causal knowledge specifies a collection of invariant or possibly invariant feature subsets, each yielding a source-trained candidate predictor. We derive matching upper and lower bounds showing that finite-sample gains are governed by the target-risk margins separating the candidates, together with the finite-source estimation error. When these margins are sufficiently large relative to $n_Q$, an adaptive aggregation procedure can match the best candidate predictor while avoiding negative transfer relative to target-only learning. On the other hand, when the margins are too small, no algorithm can reliably exploit the candidate collection to obtain faster finite-sample rates. We further connect these margins to structural shift magnitude in linear SCMs and validate the theory on real-world causal benchmarks.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how causal knowledge can enhance supervised domain adaptation (sDA) in cases of domain shifts, particularly in finite-sample settings using linear regression.
AI.motivation: Machine learning models often struggle when deployed on distributions different from their training data. The paper seeks to explore how causal knowledge influences adaptation in such domains specifically under a finite-sample setting.
AI.method: The authors focus on linear regression and analyze predictors derived from invariant or possibly invariant feature subsets based on causal knowledge. They establish theoretical bounds and propose an adaptive aggregation procedure to match the best predictor from the candidate collection.
AI.result: The study shows that performance improvement depends on target-risk margins and finite-sample estimation error. Gains occur if the margins are sufficiently large compared to the target sample size. Otherwise, no improvement can be achieved. The findings are validated on real-world datasets.
AI.conclusion: Causal knowledge can provide benefits in sDA under specific conditions, particularly when large target-risk margins are present. However, under smaller margins, these benefits are limited, and causal approaches may not outperform simpler, target-only learning methods.

title: GRIP: Feedback-Guided Prompt Retrieval for Large Multimodal Models
summary:
In-Context Learning (ICL) has become a powerful mechanism for adapting Large Language Models (LLMs) to new tasks without fine-tuning. Extending this concept to Large Multimodal Models (LMMs), Multimodal In-Context Learning (M-ICL) relies on retrieving relevant examples, such as images, captions, or question-answer pairs, to guide predictions across tasks like classification, captioning, and visual question answering (VQA). Most existing approaches select in-context examples based on feature-space similarity, assuming that semantically similar samples provide the most useful context. However, our systematic analysis reveals that this assumption does not always hold: visually similar examples are not necessarily those that most effectively enhance in-context learning performance.
  To address this, we propose the Guided Retrieval of In-context Prompts (GRIP), a learnable vision-only retrieval framework that leverages feedback from LMMs to identify examples that truly improve model predictions. GRIP learns to distinguish beneficial from detrimental in-context examples through contrastive training, refining retrieval beyond pure similarity. Across three multimodal tasks, namely classification, captioning, and VQA, GRIP improves consistently over similarity-based retrieval on Qwen2.5-VL-7B, with its strongest gains in classification on Idefics2-8B. Moreover, we demonstrate that retrievers trained with feedback from one open LMM can be transferred to other models without retraining, including closed-source GPT-4o and Gemini, enabling scalable and cost-efficient deployment of M-ICL. Code will be published upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces GRIP, a framework for improving Multimodal In-Context Learning (M-ICL) by retrieving not just similar but beneficial examples to enhance predictions in classification, captioning, and VQA tasks.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the limitation in M-ICL where examples are retrieved based on similarity, which doesn't always optimize performance, and introduces a new method to retrieve truly beneficial examples.
AI.method: The proposed GRIP framework uses contrastive training to learn which examples improve model predictions, leveraging feedback from LMMs and refining retrieval beyond similarity.
AI.result: GRIP consistently outperforms traditional similarity-based methods in three multimodal tasks across different LMMs, with evidence of retrievers trained on one model transferring well to others, including closed-source ones.
AI.conclusion: GRIP offers a scalable and cost-efficient method to enhance M-ICL by addressing the limitations of similarity-based retrieval, showing strong cross-model generalizability.

title: LLMs Can Better Capture Human Judgments--With the Right Prompts
summary: Are large language models (LLMs) bad at capturing human judgment? Two commonly stated limitations are that LLMs fail to capture full distributions of responses, and that their judgments are unstable across wording variations. We demonstrate simple prompting strategies that mitigate these limitations. Across two datasets--a U.S.-representative set of 144 moral scenarios and 38 moral beliefs from the International Social Survey Programme's Family and Changing Gender Roles module covering 32 countries--we show how simple elicitation techniques help improve AI-human alignment. First, prompting models to report standard deviations and response proportions recovers the full range of human responses better than common strategies. Second, ensuring scenarios are clear to human participants--as reflected in human confusion ratings--boosts model alignment, and LLMs can track human confusion ratings. At the same time, we find that LLMs' estimates of their own error are poorly calibrated, though they can predict human variability relatively well. These results suggest that asking better questions to LLMs can yield better answers.

AI.tldr: This paper explores whether large language models (LLMs) can align with human judgment, using improved prompting strategies to enhance AI-human alignment in capturing moral scenario and belief data.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address two perceived limitations of LLMs: failure to capture the full distribution of human responses and instability across wording variations.
AI.method: Using two datasets (144 U.S.-based moral scenarios and 38 global moral beliefs), the authors applied simple elicitation techniques such as prompting for standard deviations, response proportions, and aligning scenarios based on human confusion ratings.
AI.result: The suggested prompting strategies effectively improved LLMs' ability to capture a full range of human responses and align better with human judgments, particularly in clearer scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Improved questioning and prompting methods enhance LLMs' performance in reflecting human judgment, though their self-error calibration remains limited.

title: Stubborn: A Streamlined and Unified Reinforcement Learning Framework for Robust Motion Tracking and Fall Recovery for Humanoids
summary: Recent reinforcement learning approaches have shown great promise in improving humanoid motion tracking performance and achieving fall recovery under disturbances. However, most existing works treat motion tracking and fall recovery as different tasks and require multi-stage training with specialized recovery rewards and/or separate recovery policies. Moreover, existing reinforcement learning-based methods often terminate training episodes immediately after severe tracking failures, limiting recovery-oriented exploration in unstable or fallen states. To address the above issues, we propose Stubborn, a streamlined and unified reinforcement learning framework to achieve robust humanoid motion tracking and fall recovery. Specifically, Stubborn uses an asymmetric Actor-Critic architecture and consists of three major components. First, a yaw-aligned tracking representation is adopted to reduce sensitivity to global drift and heading disturbances while preserving gravity-related balance information. Second, we introduce a Bernoulli-based probabilistic termination mechanism that enables the policy to encourage exploration of fall-recovery behaviors under varying failure modes. Third, we propose a probabilistic termination and tracking-error-driven strategy that dynamically reshapes the sampling distribution based on tracking performance, increasing the training efficiency for difficult motion segments and unstable states. Extensive comparisons with SOTA methods and ablation studies show that Stubborn achieved competitive performance, and the proposed probabilistic termination mechanism and adaptive sampling strategy contributed to the performance and robustness gains. For real-world demonstrations, please refer to https://aislab-sustech.github.io/Stubborn/.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces 'Stubborn,' a framework that unifies humanoid motion tracking and fall recovery into a single training process using reinforcement learning.
AI.motivation: Current methods separate motion tracking and fall recovery as distinct tasks, requiring multi-stage training and specific setups. These methods also limit exploration after severe tracking failures, hindering effective fall recovery training.
AI.method: The authors propose a unified framework with an asymmetric Actor-Critic model, yaw-aligned representations to handle disturbances, a probabilistic termination mechanism for exploration, and an adaptive sampling strategy to focus on challenging states.
AI.result: The framework demonstrates competitive performance compared to state-of-the-art methods. The novel probabilistic termination mechanism and sampling strategy significantly enhance robustness and efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Stubborn provides a streamlined and effective approach for robust humanoid motion tracking and fall recovery, addressing key limitations of existing methods.

title: Net-Ev$^2$: A Generative Simulator for Network Event Evolution
summary: Reducing real-world trial and error has long been a central goal of decision making, and generative simulators advance this goal by modeling the evolution of future states. An even more challenging yet meaningful task is simulating how disturbance events (e.g., accidents) propagate their impacts across real-world networks. The existing approaches fall short of modeling both structured attributes and unstructured semantics of events, and capturing topological structures in simulating network event evolution. Therefore, we are motivated to propose Net-Ev$^2$ ($\underline{\textbf{Net}}$work $\underline{\textbf{Ev}}$ent $\underline{\textbf{Ev}}$olution), a novel generative simulator that jointly leverages event cues while preserving network topology in simulations. Specifically, the framework consists of two stages, namely structure-guided masked pre-training and topology-aware diffusion process, which is achieved by U-Net-like graph downsampling and upsampling during denoising. At inference time, Net-Ev$^2$ can generate simulations using natural-language event input only, with greater flexibility for practical usage. Furthermore, we introduce Net-Ev$^2$-6.5M, a multimodal benchmark of aligned event and network traffic data across four large-scale road networks, as well as a new topology-aware metric, namely JL-MMD, to evaluate topological fidelity in generated network dynamics. Extensive experiments demonstrate the state-of-the-art performance and strong generalization ability of Net-Ev$^2$. Code is made available at https://github.com/Guangyu4/Net-Ev-2.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Net-Ev$^2$, a generative simulator that models how disturbances affect real-world networks, focusing on structured attribute modeling and topological fidelity.
AI.motivation: The current methods inadequately integrate structured attributes, unstructured event semantics, and topological structures when simulating event impacts on networks.
AI.method: The framework employs two stages: structure-guided masked pre-training and a topology-aware diffusion process using U-Net-like graph downsampling and upsampling.
AI.result: Net-Ev$^2$ achieves state-of-the-art performance and generalization in network event simulations, validated with the new Net-Ev$^2$-6.5M dataset and the JL-MMD metric.
AI.conclusion: Net-Ev$^2$ offers a robust and flexible solution for simulating real-world event impacts on networks, enabling enhanced predictive modeling and decision-making.

title: From Real-World Projects to Research-Oriented Learning: Continuous Improvement of a Master-Level Course in Software Engineering Education
summary: Problem: Despite growing interest in project-based learning, little is known about how a master-level course can be continuously evolved toward research-oriented approaches over several years and how students perceive this development. Method: We conducted a longitudinal mixed-methods study of a master-level course in Information Systems at the University of Applied Sciences and Arts Hannover (Germany). The analysis covers six years between 2019 and 2025 and draws on teaching evaluations, course documentation, and reflective teaching artifacts. Results: The course evolved from a practice-oriented project format toward a more explicitly research-oriented learning environment. Despite this change, students' perceived course quality remained positive. Authentic projects, external collaboration, lecturer support, structured scaffolding, and visible relevance supported positive student perceptions. Contribution: This paper shows how a master-level course can be continuously evolved toward research-oriented learning while maintaining positive student perceptions. It further identifies the course design decisions that supported this transition.

AI.tldr: This study analyzes the evolution of a master-level course from being practice-oriented to research-oriented while maintaining positive student perceptions over six years.
AI.motivation: To understand how a master-level course can shift towards research-oriented approaches while maintaining student satisfaction and to identify the course design elements that enable this transformation.
AI.method: A longitudinal mixed-methods study covering six years (2019-2025) was conducted using teaching evaluations, course documentation, and reflective teaching artifacts.
AI.result: The course successfully transitioned to a research-oriented format, and student perceptions of the course remained positive due to factors like authentic projects, external collaboration, lecturer support, structured scaffolding, and visible relevance.
AI.conclusion: A master-level course can evolve toward research-oriented learning without compromising positive student perceptions, aided by key design choices that promote engagement and relevance.

title: The Theory of Mind Utility: Formal Specification of a Mentalizing Mechanism
summary: Inferring others' beliefs requires more than reading surface signals; it requires tracking who told them what, in what order, and how credibly. The Theory of Mind Utility (ToM-U) formalizes this epistemic state inference problem at the computational level of analysis, specifying what mentalizing computes and why without commitment to algorithmic or neural implementation. ToM-U achieves this by constructing Local Epistemic World Models (LEWMs) -- directed typed graphs that represent agents, state nodes, and the epistemic relationships among them -- and evaluating discrete candidate LEWMs against observed behavior until one achieves sufficient confidence. Five formal definitions specify the LEWM structure, agent node properties including ordered information access history, a bounded proliferation mechanism for recursive mentalizing, three inference procedures, and a residue function that captures the structured trace left by failed mentalizing attempts. ToM-U differs from Bayesian Theory of Mind and adjacent formal accounts, which presuppose rather than derive belief states, and from simulation theory and theory-theory, which lack a formal apparatus for epistemic state inference. The architecture generates directional, falsifiable predictions about mentalizing failure that follow from structural properties of the model rather than auxiliary assumptions, and positions ToM-U as a domain-agnostic mechanism upstream of goal inference and other downstream social cognitive processes.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Theory of Mind Utility (ToM-U), a framework for understanding how individuals infer the beliefs of others at a computational level. It uses Local Epistemic World Models (LEWMs) and formal definitions to evaluate this process.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to develop a computational-level framework for inferring others' beliefs by addressing limitations in existing theories like Bayesian Theory of Mind, simulation theory, and theory-theory.
AI.method: The framework operates by building LEWMs (directed typed graphs), specifying five formal definitions, introducing recursive mentalizing mechanisms, and evaluating these models against observed behavior.
AI.result: ToM-U distinguishes itself by generating directional, testable predictions about mentalizing failures without relying on auxiliary assumptions, offering a domain-agnostic mechanism.
AI.conclusion: ToM-U redefines epistemic state inference by deriving belief states via LEWMs and provides a robust alternative to traditional theories, with applications in understanding downstream social cognitive processes.

title: Two-Layer Linear Auto-Regressive Models Estimate Latent States
summary: Auto-regressive models have emerged as powerful tools for sequential data, from language to video. Understanding how and why these models learn latent representations remains an open theoretical question. In this work, we demonstrate that when trained by empirical risk minimization on data from partially observed linear dynamical systems, two-layer linear auto-regressive models naturally learn to approximate Kalman filtering. In particular, we show that the learned hidden representation coincides, up to a similarity transformation, with the state estimates produced by the optimal (Kalman) filter, even though the model has no explicit knowledge of the underlying dynamics or state. The result follows from three main insights. First, we establish that the Kalman filter is well approximated by an auto-regressive model with bounded truncation error. Second, we show that despite non-convexity, the two-layer optimization landscape is benign, i.e., all stationary points are either strict saddles or global minima. Finally, as our main contributions, we provide finite-sample guarantees on prediction error, parameter estimation error, and latent state recovery. Numerical simulations support the theoretical results and demonstrate that the latent representations of auto-regressive models recover state estimates.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes two-layer linear auto-regressive models and demonstrates that these models naturally approximate Kalman filtering under specific conditions.
AI.motivation: The study seeks to understand how and why auto-regressive models learn latent representations, which remains a theoretical gap in sequential data processing.
AI.method: The authors theoretically and empirically show that these models approximate Kalman filters by analyzing optimization landscapes and providing finite-sample guarantees for prediction and state recovery.
AI.result: The main findings confirm that auto-regressive models recover the latent state estimates produced by Kalman filters, supported by theoretical insights and numerical simulations.
AI.conclusion: Two-layer linear auto-regressive models can approximate Kalman filters naturally, even without explicit knowledge of underlying dynamics, offering a deeper understanding of their latent learning mechanisms.

title: DIMOS: Disentangling Instance-level Moving Object Segmentation
summary: Moving instance segmentation (MIS) attracts increasing attention due to its broad applications in traffic surveillance, autonomous driving, and animal tracking. Event cameras record asynchronous brightness changes, providing high temporal resolution and dynamic range, which makes them highly sensitive to motion information. By fusing event and image features, motion cues from events can complement spatial details from images, enhancing the performance of MIS. However, current multimodal MIS methods still struggle to segment small moving instances, as event cameras often yield sparse features under limited resolution. Moreover, event features entangle appearance attributes with motion cues, which further restricts effective cross-modal fusion. To address these challenges, we first propose a dual-disentangling feature extraction framework that separates and extracts appearance and motion information within both image and event modalities, thereby improving feature density. Subsequently, a multi-granularity cross-modal alignment is introduced to align distributionally and semantically consistent features across modalities, enabling more effective fusion with rich spatial and temporal details.
