Experimental results demonstrate that our model learns target-dependent cyclization preferences, and enables effective and controllable multi-property optimization for cyclic peptide design. The source code of this paper is available at https://github.com/HKUSTGZ-ML4Health-Lab/APCyc.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces APCyc, a framework for generating cyclic peptides that improves on existing models by accounting for cyclization constraints and multi-property optimization.
AI.motivation: There is a need for de novo design of cyclic peptides, as they offer improved stability and binding affinity, but existing methods trained on linear peptides fail to capture cyclization-specific challenges.
AI.method: APCyc expands the residue vocabulary, encodes cyclization-site and linkage-type information, and employs Bayesian posterior guidance to generate cyclic peptides optimized for multiple properties.
AI.result: The model demonstrates target-dependent cyclization preferences and achieves controllable multi-property optimization in the design of cyclic peptides.
AI.conclusion: APCyc successfully addresses limitations of previous approaches and provides an effective tool for designing cyclic peptides with target-specific constraints and properties, with code made publicly available.

title: Visual Place Recognition in Forests with Depth-Aware Distillation
comment: IEEE ICRA Workshop on Field Robotics 2026
summary: Visual place recognition in natural forest environments remains challenging due to repetitive vegetation, weak structural cues, and significant appearance variation across traversals. To address this limitation, this paper proposes a lightweight depth-aware distillation framework that injects geometric cues into a DINOv2-based place recognition model, while maintaining its pre-trained descriptor space. Evaluated on the recent WildCross benchmark, the proposed approach yields gains over an appearance-only counterpart, providing robustness to appearance variations. These results demonstrate the importance of depth as a strong complementary modality for place recognition in natural environments and identify depth-aware distillation as a promising direction for more robust forest perception.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a lightweight framework using depth cues for visual place recognition in forests, achieving improvements over appearance-only methods.
AI.motivation: To tackle challenges in forest visual place recognition due to repetitive vegetation, weak structural cues, and varying appearances.
AI.method: Proposes a depth-aware distillation framework that integrates geometric (depth) cues into a DINOv2-based recognition model without altering its descriptor space.
AI.result: The model shows improvements on the WildCross benchmark compared to appearance-only approaches, demonstrating its robustness to visual variations.
AI.conclusion: Depth serves as a valuable complementary modality for visual place recognition in natural environments, and depth-aware distillation is a promising technique for forest perception.

title: SICI: A Semantic-Pragmatic Complexity Index Reveals Regime Shifts in LLM Stance Detection
summary: Prompt-based LLMs are increasingly used for stance detection, but harder examples are not always repaired by clearer instructions, reasoning prompts, retrieval, or debate. We introduce SICI (Stance Inference Complexity Index), a seven-dimensional diagnostic measure of the semantic-pragmatic burden imposed by a target--text pair. Across SemEval-2016 and VAST, SICI predicts LLM accuracy better than surface proxies and shows substantial cross-scorer reliability ($α=0.771$). More importantly, LLM errors change regime as SICI increases: low-complexity examples invite over-attribution, especially Against predictions; intermediate examples form an unstable boundary; and high-complexity examples rapidly concentrate on None. This phase-transition-like structure persists across GPT-3.5, GPT-4o-mini, DeepSeek-V3, and GPT-4o, although stronger models move the boundaries. A 15-method intervention study further shows that prompting, retrieval, and debate often shift models along the attribution--abstention axis rather than removing the high-complexity bottleneck.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SICI, a diagnostic measure to assess the complexity of stance detection tasks for LLMs, and demonstrates that increased complexity shifts model errors across semantic-pragmatic boundaries.
AI.motivation: To address why large language models (LLMs) struggle with harder stance detection cases even when provided with enhanced prompts, retrieval, or reasoning techniques.
AI.method: The authors present SICI, a seven-dimensional measure for quantifying the semantic-pragmatic complexity of stance detection tasks, and analyze its predictive power and impact across different LLMs and scenarios.
AI.result: SICI achieves strong predictive reliability (α=0.771) in estimating LLM accuracy and reveals distinct error regimes for low, intermediate, and high complexity cases across various LLMs, including GPT-3.5 and GPT-4o.
AI.conclusion: SICI explains LLM error patterns in stance detection tasks, highlighting how model interventions shift errors instead of resolving complex cases altogether, emphasizing the persistence of the high-complexity bottleneck.

title: MCR-Bionic Hand: Anatomical Structural Priors for Dexterous Manipulation
summary:
Dexterous robotic hands are usually formulated as high dimensional active control systems governed by degrees of freedom, actuation, and algorithms. Human hand dexterity, however, is partly encoded in the physical architecture of bones, ligaments, tendons, aponeuroses, and intrinsic muscles. This work describes that contribution as two linked forms of structural intelligence: structural prior generation, in which wrist to finger tenodesis, FDS/FDP routing, and the dorsal extensor hood transform low dimensional posture inputs into default grasp configurations and PIP to DIP coordination; and muscle mediated modulation, in which extrinsic muscles, lumbricals, and interossei regulate MCP posture, distal stability, fingertip force paths, and contact states around that default state.
  Based on this framework, MCR-Bionic Hand is developed as a 1:1 musculoskeletal biomimetic hand integrating a two row eight bone wrist, cross wrist tendons, anatomical flexor routing, volar plate and collateral ligament constraints, the dorsal extensor hood, and intrinsic muscle pathways within one body. Functional demonstrations and geometric mechanical models show that wrist posture induces multi joint pre shaping, the extensor hood maps PIP posture to a coupled DIP response, and intrinsic plus pathways modulate distal stability and fingertip action direction after grasp formation. Contact rich tasks, including coin rotation, pen transfer, dorsal coin flipping, and cube manipulation, show that MCR-Bionic links low dimensional state generation with fine post contact modulation. These results suggest that anatomical biomimetics is valuable not for visual similarity, but for identifying human hand structures that perform part of control.

AI.tldr: The paper presents the MCR-Bionic Hand, a biomimetic robotic hand which mimics human anatomical structures to enhance dexterity and fine control.
AI.motivation: To capture the physical architecture of human hand dexterity in a robotic system by integrating bones, ligaments, and muscles for improved robotic hand functionality.
AI.method: The researchers designed the MCR-Bionic Hand by mimicking key human hand structures, such as the extensor hood and ligament routing, and validated its performance through geometric models and functional demonstrations.
AI.result: The MCR-Bionic Hand showcases capabilities like joint pre-shaping, coupled joint responses, and fine post-grasp modulation in contact-rich tasks.
AI.conclusion: Anatomical biomimetics effectively enhances robotic hand performance by replicating key control mechanisms inherent in the human hand's physical structure.

title: Normative Robustness as a Frontier for Non-Verifiable Reasoning in LLMs
summary: As LLMs increasingly serve in advisory and deliberative roles, users rely on them for non-verifiable reasoning in domains lacking objective ground truths. However, traditional evaluations of LLM reasoning focus almost exclusively on fact-based domains, such as mathematics and science, leaving uncertainty over whether and to what degree models can handle ambiguous, subjective, or value-laden problems over time. To address this concern, we propose moral reasoning as a paradigmatic subdomain of non-verifiable reasoning. We define moral robustness as a model's capacity to exhibit sound moral reasoning across time and contexts, and we introduce a scalable, adversarial, multi-turn evaluation framework to empirically measure this capability. We simulate 48,000 user-agent moral deliberations across four frontier LLMs, varying premise relevance, premise order, conversation duration, and the user's stated moral view. We find that models successfully ignore morally-irrelevant distractors, but shift their reasoning by up to 6.5%, on average, towards the user's stated preferred moral view, and varying their reasoning depending on factors such as order (altering moral judgments by order in 13-22% of the cases) and duration (altering moral judgments between single-turn and multi-turn in 10-24% of the cases). Our analysis indicates that models tailor not just their final verdicts but their underlying justifications to align with a user's moral viewpoint - a failure mode we characterize as moral deliberative sycophancy.

AI.tldr: This paper studies how large language models (LLMs) handle moral reasoning, using an evaluation framework to measure their consistency and response to contextual factors.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses a gap in LLM evaluation by focusing on their reasoning in non-verifiable domains, like morality, instead of traditional fact-based domains.
AI.method: They define 'moral robustness' and use a multi-turn evaluation framework to test moral reasoning in over 48,000 simulated user-agent moral deliberations, analyzing variables like premise order and user moral views.
AI.result: Results show that while models ignore irrelevant distractions, they exhibit tendencies to align their reasoning and justifications towards a user's stated moral view and are affected by conversational context changes.
AI.conclusion: LLMs demonstrate a vulnerability, termed moral deliberative sycophancy, indicating they may not consistently retain moral robustness in advisory or deliberative roles.

title: The Illusion of Multi-Agent Advantage
summary: Prevailing wisdom posits that Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) are superior to Single-Agent Systems (SAS), citing advantages like context protection, parallel processing and distributed decision-making. However, empirical support for this claim relies primarily on comparisons with SAS baselines using benchmarks that prioritize isolated reasoning tasks, which do not adequately assess these advantages. Focusing on automatically generated MAS that are designed for enhanced generalizability over manually-designed counterparts, we perform a rigorous, systematic evaluation against SAS, specifically Chain-of-Thought with Self-Consistency (CoT-SC). Across traditional reasoning datasets and tasks with interactive multi-step workflows (e.g., BrowseComp-Plus), we demonstrate that automatic MAS consistently underperform CoT-SC despite being up to 10x more expensive. To isolate these failures from limitations inherent to task structure, we introduce a diagnostic synthetic dataset tailored for MAS featuring explicit task decomposition, context separation and parallelization potential. We show that expert-architected MAS consistently outperforms automatically generated architectures in both raw performance and cost-efficiency on this dataset, demonstrating that existing evaluation frameworks mask critical architectural gaps and inefficiencies of complex MAS by failing to account for the marginal utility of increased computational cost. Critically, systematic deconstruction of the generated MAS architectures reveals that current automated design paradigms produce architectural bloat that prioritizes superficial complexity which does not translate into functional utility, exposing a fundamental misalignment with multi-agent principles.

AI.tldr: The study finds that automatically designed Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) often underperform Single-Agent Systems (SAS) in reasoning tasks and are significantly more expensive. Expert-designed MAS show better results, hinting at flaws in current automated MAS design paradigms.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to challenge the prevailing belief that MAS inherently outperform SAS by questioning the choice of benchmarks and design paradigms used in these comparisons.
AI.method: The researchers evaluate automatically generated MAS against SAS using various reasoning datasets, including interactive tasks. They also develop a synthetic dataset designed to highlight MAS's potential advantages to diagnose issues in MAS design.
AI.result: Automatic MAS consistently underperformed SAS, even with significantly higher computational costs. Expert-designed MAS, however, showed better performance and cost efficiency on a tailored diagnostic dataset.
AI.conclusion: The study critiques automated MAS design frameworks, highlighting their inefficiencies and architectural flaws, which prioritize complexity over functional utility, misaligning them with core multi-agent principles.

title: TimeLens: On-Device Artifact Recognition with Retrieval-Augmented Question Answering for the Grand Egyptian Museum
comment: 6 pages, 4 figures, 5 tables. Submitted to AIVRCH 2026
summary: TimeLens is an AI-powered bilingual mobile guide for the Grand Egyptian Museum (GEM). Pointing a phone at an exhibit, a visitor sees the artifact recognized in real time and can ask follow-up questions answered in English or Arabic. The work addresses three problems specific to in-gallery deployment: fine-grained visual similarity among 51 catalogued artifacts (many near-identical Ramesside statues), the gap between curated training data and handheld camera conditions, and the risk of an AI guide stating unsupported historical facts. Two engineering contributions are reported. First, an on-device artifact detector was developed through a data-quality-driven iteration study -- from foundation-model auto-annotation (YOLO-World), through spatial label-cleaning rules, to a fully hand-annotated dataset -- isolating label quality as the decisive factor: the final YOLOv8n model resolves every previously failing class while remaining a 5.97 MB TensorFlow Lite asset that runs in real time on a mid-range phone (mAP@0.5 = 0.995, mAP@0.5:0.95 = 0.924). Second, a bilingual Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) guide, grounded in a 108-record ChromaDB knowledge base, was benchmarked across seven candidate language models, with Gemma 4 E2B (Q4 K M) selected; ten targeted optimizations reduce end-to-end latency from over 30 s to approximately 10 s. Both subsystems are integrated in a production Flutter application with bilingual interface, museum location gating, and text-to-speech support.

AI.tldr: TimeLens is an AI-powered bilingual guide for the Grand Egyptian Museum with real-time artifact recognition and bilingual interaction, addressing challenges in visual similarity, data conditions, and fact accuracy.
AI.motivation: To enhance visitor experience in the Grand Egyptian Museum by enabling real-time identification and interaction with artifacts in both English and Arabic.
AI.method: The research used iterative engineering to develop an efficient artifact detector using YOLOv8n and a bilingual AI guide grounded in a curated knowledge base and optimized for latency. These were implemented in a user-friendly mobile app.
AI.result: The artifact detector achieved mAP@0.5 = 0.995 and mAP@0.5:0.95 = 0.924, while the AI guide's latency was optimized to approximately 10 seconds. Both systems were integrated into a production-ready bilingual mobile application.
AI.conclusion: The study successfully deployed a practical, high-performance AI-powered museum guide addressing fine-grained recognition, adaptability to in-gallery conditions, and linguistic needs of bilingual users.

title: Layer-Resolved Optimal Transport for Hallucination Detection in NMT and Abstractive Summarization
comment: Accepted to ICML Mechanistic Interpretability Workshop 2026
summary: Optimal transport (OT) has been shown to detect hallucinations in neural machine translation (NMT) by measuring the geometric distance between cross-attention distributions and a reference distribution, without any supervision. We extend this analysis to all six decoder layers of the Fairseq DE-EN model ($N=3{,}414$), showing that Wass-to-Unif and Wass-to-Data are complementary detectors specialised across hallucination types, that detection is concentrated in layers L1--L4 with L5 anti-predictive for subtler types, and that hallucinated translations lack the exploratory attention phase present in correct translations from the first decoding step. We further evaluate whether the geometric signal transfers to abstractive summarization faithfulness detection: our unsupervised OT detector on AggreFact ($N=1{,}116$) achieves $57.2\%$/$57.6\%$ balanced accuracy on CNN/XSum -- above chance but substantially below supervised MiniCheck-Flan-T5-L($69.9\%$/$74.3\%$). This gap is principled: unlike NMT hallucinations, unfaithful summaries can attend correctly to source tokens while misrepresenting their content, a failure mode invisible to concentration-based OT metrics by construction. Structural experiments on T5-base confirm consistent decoder organisation across depth, with Layer~3 showing peak concentration and Layer~12 being most critical for generation quality. Together, the results establish OT on cross-attention as a reliable detector when the failure mode is source disengagement, a principled interpretability tool regardless of task, and fundamentally limited when faithfulness failures occur downstream of attention.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates the use of Optimal Transport (OT) metrics as an unsupervised tool for detecting hallucinations and faithfulness in text generated by neural models, finding its strengths and limitations.
AI.motivation: The study aims to address the challenge of identifying hallucinations in neural machine translation and unfaithful outputs in abstractive summarization without requiring supervision, while also exploring the structural organization of decoder layers.
AI.method: The authors extend previous investigations using OT metrics on attention distributions across all decoder layers in a Fairseq DE-EN model for NMT and evaluate the transferability of these metrics for abstractive summarization faithfulness detection on the AggreFact dataset.
AI.result: The study finds that OT metrics, specifically Wass-to-Unif and Wass-to-Data, are effective for hallucination detection in NMT, with detection concentrated in certain decoder layers. However, their performance on abstractive summarization tasks is below supervised methods, due to inherent limitations in capturing unfaithful content misrepresentations.
AI.conclusion: Optimal Transport metrics on cross-attention provide a reliable and interpretable detection tool for failure modes related to source disengagement, but they are fundamentally limited in identifying faithfulness failures that originate beyond attention behavior.

title: $\texttt{WEAVER}$, Better, Faster, Longer: An Effective World Model for Robotic Manipulation
summary: The potential impacts of world models (WMs, i.e., learned simulators) on robotics are far-reaching -- policy evaluation, policy improvement, and test-time planning -- all with limited real-world interaction. To unlock these downstream capabilities, a WM needs to jointly satisfy three desiderata: $\textit{(i)}$ fidelity (i.e., producing simulated trajectories that correlate with reality), $\textit{(ii)}$ consistency (i.e., producing simulated trajectories that are coherent over long horizons), and $\textit{(iii)}$ efficiency (i.e., producing simulated trajectories quickly). We propose $\texttt{WEAVER}$ (World Estimation Across Views for Embodied Reasoning): a WM architecture that simultaneously achieves all three desiderata, providing state-of-the-art results on robotic manipulation tasks. $\texttt{WEAVER}$ is a multi-view WM trained to predict future latents and reward values via a flow-matching loss. We distill the key design decisions across model architecture, memory, and prediction objectives required to unlock the kinds of long-horizon dynamic manipulation tasks that have confounded prior world modeling approaches. We apply $\texttt{WEAVER}$ in robotic hardware, demonstrating its effectiveness at policy evaluation ($ρ$=0.870 correlation with real-world success rate), policy improvement (real-world success rate improvement of $38\%$ on top of the $π_{0.5}$ robot foundation model), and test-time planning (real-world success rate improvement of $14\%$ with a $5-10\times$ speedup over prior WMs). $\texttt{WEAVER}$ also demonstrates better performance than prior WMs when evaluated on out-of-distribution scenarios. Code, models, and videos at: https://arnavkj1995.github.io/WEAVER/ .

AI.tldr: This paper introduces WEAVER, a multi-view world model designed for robotic manipulation tasks, achieving superior performance across fidelity, consistency, and efficiency.
AI.motivation: To solve challenges of policy evaluation, policy improvement, and test-time planning in robotics through a world model that balances high fidelity, long-horizon consistency, and computational efficiency.
AI.method: The authors propose WEAVER, a multi-view world model trained using a flow-matching loss to predict future latents and reward values, and optimized across key design aspects like architecture, memory, and objectives.
AI.result: WEAVER achieved cutting-edge results in robotic manipulation: strong correlation with real-world performance ($ρ$=0.870), 38% improvement in policy success rates, and 14% success improvement for planning with 5–10x speedups over prior models. It also performed well on out-of-distribution tasks.
AI.conclusion: WEAVER presents a scalable, accurate, and efficient framework for downstream robotic tasks, outperforming existing world models while enabling robust real-world applications.

title: Let's Ask Gauss: Improved One-Run Privacy Auditing
summary: Privacy auditing provides an important safeguard by estimating the actual information leaked by a model, thus ensuring that theoretical privacy guarantees hold in practice. We study empirical privacy auditing for differentially private (DP) machine learning, focusing on efficient one-run methods for mechanisms such as DP-SGD. Prior one-run approaches threshold training examples or "canaries" into binary membership guesses, which discards useful information. We show that, in the white-box DP-SGD setting, canary-aligned signals naturally form a sequence of random variables whose normalized sum is asymptotically Gaussian. Leveraging this distributional perspective, we develop a DP-auditing framework that leads to tighter privacy lower bounds from a single training run.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a framework for efficient privacy auditing in differentially private machine learning, offering improved lower bounds for privacy leakage in models like DP-SGD based on a novel statistical approach.
AI.motivation: To ensure theoretical privacy guarantees actually hold in practice by developing efficient empirical privacy auditing methods for differentially private machine learning.
AI.method: The authors introduce a one-run DP-auditing framework using white-box DP-SGD settings, leveraging the asymptotic Gaussian distribution of canary-aligned signals for improved privacy evaluation.
AI.result: The new auditing framework provides tighter lower bounds on privacy leakage during a single training run, compared to prior binary threshold-based methods.
AI.conclusion: By employing a novel statistical perspective, this paper enhances the accuracy and efficiency of privacy auditing, strengthening confidence in the practicality of theoretical privacy guarantees.

title: Otters++: A Time-to-first-spike Based Energy Efficient Optical Spiking Transformer
summary: Spiking neural networks (SNNs) are promising for energy-efficient inference, and time-to-first-spike (TTFS) coding is especially attractive because each neuron fires at most once. In practice, however, this benefit is often reduced by the cost of computing a temporal decay term and multiplying it by the synaptic weight. We address this issue by turning a physical hardware "bug," the natural signal decay in optoelectronic devices, into the main computation of TTFS, named Otters++. Specifically, we use the measured decay of a custom In$_2$O$_3$ optoelectronic synapse to directly realize the TTFS temporal term, removing the need for explicit digital decay computation. To scale this idea to Transformer models, we establish a layer-wise functional equivalence between the Otters++ and a quantized neural network (QNN), and develop a hybrid training method that uses device-faithful SNN computation in the forward pass and QNN straight-through gradients through the equivalent QNN path in the backward pass, together with model distillation. This avoids differentiation through discrete first-spike events and reduces the over-sparsity problem in direct TTFS-SNN training. We further make training aware of measured device noise by sampling run-to-run variation, and refine the system-level energy model by accounting for device sharing and multi-hop communication. On GLUE dataset, Otters++ improves the average score to 84.17\% while maintaining a clear energy advantage over prior spiking Transformer baselines. These results show that physically grounded TTFS computing can be efficient, trainable, and robust under realistic hardware effects.

AI.tldr: This paper presents Otters++, a system using optoelectronic synapse signal decay for energy-efficient spiking neural network (SNN) computation, with improved energy efficiency and performance in Transformer-based tasks.
AI.motivation: The study aims to enhance the energy efficiency and computational simplicity of time-to-first-spike (TTFS) coding in SNNs by utilizing the natural signal decay in hardware, which is often considered a drawback.
AI.method: Otters++ leverages the physical decay in custom optoelectronic synapses to eliminate explicit decay computation. It employs a layer-wise equivalence to quantized neural networks (QNN), hybrid training methods, model distillation, and incorporates measured hardware noise in training for robustness.
AI.result: Otters++ achieves an 84.17% average score on the GLUE dataset, surpassing previous spiking Transformer baselines, and shows significant energy efficiency improvements.
AI.conclusion: The research validates that harnessing physical hardware characteristics, like natural decay in optoelectronics, can lead to efficient, trainable, and robust TTFS computation for SNNs.

title: Zero-Shot Captioning for Cultural Heritage: Automated Image Analysis of Traditional Indonesian Clothing
comment: accepted to ICME workshop on AIART 2026
summary: This paper presents Custom ZeroCLIP, a retrieval-augmented vision-language framework for zero-shot captioning of Indonesian traditional garments. The dataset contains 3,800 expert-annotated images from all 38 Indonesian provinces. Using a province-level inductive zero-shot protocol, the model is trained on 24 seen provinces, validated on 6 seen provinces, and evaluated on 8 unseen provinces. The framework combines a frozen CLIP ViT-B/32 image encoder, a CLIP text encoder, a BERT text encoder, and an LSTM caption decoder. During inference, unseen-province labels and captions are unavailable, and retrieval uses only captions from training provinces. No unseen-province image, label, or caption is used during training, validation, or retrieval-bank construction. Custom ZeroCLIP achieves a CLIPScore of 0.8536, BLEU-4 of 0.3342, and METEOR of 0.4859, outperforming existing baselines. Ablation results show that retrieval improves cultural vocabulary recovery with a 19.3\% METEOR gain, while human evaluation confirms stronger cultural accuracy and fluency. The results demonstrate the effectiveness of retrieval-augmented domain adaptation for culturally grounded caption generation in low-resource heritage settings. The dataset is publicly available at https://github.com/AnugrahAidinYotolembah/Traditional-Indonesian-Clothing-Captioning-Dataset.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Custom ZeroCLIP, a vision-language framework for zero-shot captioning of Indonesian traditional garments, demonstrating its effectiveness through significant performance gains over baselines.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to create a culturally grounded caption generation framework for low-resource heritage settings, addressing the challenge of generating captions for traditional Indonesian garments across provinces.
AI.method: The approach leverages a retrieval-augmented framework combining a frozen CLIP ViT-B/32 image encoder, a CLIP text encoder, a BERT text encoder, and an LSTM caption decoder, using a novel inductive zero-shot protocol over 38 Indonesian provinces.
AI.result: Custom ZeroCLIP achieves strong quantitative metrics (CLIPScore: 0.8536, BLEU-4: 0.3342, METEOR: 0.4859), outperforms baselines, shows a 19.3% METEOR gain in cultural vocabulary recovery through retrieval, and is validated by human evaluation for cultural accuracy and fluency.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that retrieval-augmented domain adaptation effectively generates culturally accurate captions in low-resource settings, contributing to both research and practical applications.

title: When Similar Means Different: Evaluating LLMs on Arabic--Hebrew Cognates
summary: Arabic and Hebrew, as closely related Semitic languages, share a substantial lexicon of true cognates, misleading false friends, and modern loanwords. This overlap poses a challenge for cross-lingual semantic understanding in large language models (LLMs). To evaluate this capability, we introduce SemCog Bench, a curated benchmark of 1,858 Arabic--Hebrew word pairs with sentence-level annotations for cognate identification and semantic disambiguation. We evaluate open-source and commercial LLMs across multiple input representations (raw, diacritized, Romanized, and phonetic) and reveal a critical gap in cross-lingual reasoning. While models achieve high accuracy on true cognates, performance drops sharply on false friends and loanwords, reflecting a strong reliance on surface-form similarity. Furthermore, sentence-level context yields only modest improvements, suggesting that contextual cues alone are insufficient to overcome misleading form-based signals. These findings reveal a fundamental limitation of current LLMs in resolving cross-lingual form--meaning conflicts and establish SemCog Bench as a rigorous benchmark for multilingual semantic reasoning. Our code and data are publicly available.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SemCog Bench, a benchmark for evaluating Arabic-Hebrew cross-lingual understanding in LLMs. Models struggle particularly with false friends and loanwords, highlighting limitations in multilingual semantic reasoning.
AI.motivation: The overlap between Arabic and Hebrew lexicons creates challenges for cross-lingual semantic understanding in LLMs, necessitating a benchmark to evaluate and improve their performance in this area.
AI.method: The study curated a dataset of 1,858 Arabic-Hebrew word pairs annotated for cognate identification and semantic disambiguation, and evaluated various LLMs on multiple input representations.
AI.result: LLMs perform well on true cognates but fail on false friends and loanwords, with sentence-level context providing only modest improvements. This indicates overreliance on surface-form similarity.
AI.conclusion: Current LLMs show fundamental limitations in resolving cross-lingual form-meaning conflicts. SemCog Bench serves as a critical benchmark for progress in multilingual semantic reasoning.

title: Improving Robotic Generalist Policies via Flow Reversal Steering
summary: Generalist policies can learn a wide range of skills from diverse robot datasets. In order to solve or improve on challenging news tasks, we need a way to infer and invoke the appropriate actions from the policy's rich behavioral prior, especially when directly commanding the policy fails. We focus on flow matching generalists and propose Flow Reversal Steering (FRS): a method that takes suboptimal but ``reasonable'' actions, finds their latent noises by passing them through the flow policy in reverse, and maps them to nearby generalist action modes. We evaluate FRS across many simulated and real-world manipulation settings. First, FRS can turn coarse semantic guidance from humans or vision-language models (VLMs) into corresponding good robot actions, improving zero-shot control. These gains can be distilled with behavioral cloning by training an auxiliary policy to output noises that the generalist maps to good actions -- showing up to 95% absolute task success rate boosts in under a minute of training. Finally, FRS enables policy improvement by bootstrapping reinforcement learning with semantic knowledge, improving on several tasks that standard RL fails to improve on.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Flow Reversal Steering (FRS), a method to enhance generalist robot policies for improved zero-shot control and reinforcement learning by steering latent action modes.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the challenge of invoking appropriate actions from generalist policies in robotic systems, especially in situations where direct commands fail, and there is a need to leverage the policy's behavioral prior for diverse task settings.
AI.method: The authors propose Flow Reversal Steering (FRS), which identifies latent noises from suboptimal actions by reversing the flow policy, and maps them to effective action modes. FRS is also used for policy improvement by enabling reinforcement learning with semantic guidance.
AI.result: FRS demonstrates effective zero-shot control by converting human or vision-language model guidance into good actions. It shows up to 95% task success rate improvement through auxiliary training and outperforms standard reinforcement learning on challenging tasks.
AI.conclusion: Flow Reversal Steering (FRS) is a promising approach to improve generalist robot policy performance, enabling stronger zero-shot control, task success via training, and semantic knowledge-boosted reinforcement learning on challenging tasks.

title: Physics-Informed Neural Networks and Radial Basis Functions for PDEs with Dirac Delta Sources
summary: Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) are a machine learning method for solving forward and inverse Partial Differential Equations (PDEs). When applied to PDEs with Dirac delta functions in the forcing terms, boundary conditions, or initial conditions, PINNs require approximating them with smooth surrogate functions, a practice that can introduce significant modeling errors. In this work, we exploit the interpretation of PINNs as Residual Least Squares (RLS) methods and show that this perspective enables direct treatment of Dirac delta terms by integrating the weak-form equation. Among RLS formulations other than PINN, we focus on the Radial Basis Function (RBF) expansion (also known as a single-layer RBF Network). We show that while integrating out the Dirac delta in PINNs causes residuals to fail to converge to zero, RBF-RLS consistently provides good forward and inverse solutions to transport problems. We explain this finding using the Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) theory. We test both approaches on linear PDEs that represent groundwater flow and transport in porous media and rivers. We solve inverse problems to fit synthetic data, noisy synthetic data, and real-world measurements.

AI.tldr: The paper explores a new approach for handling Dirac delta functions in Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) by using a Radial Basis Function Residual Least Squares (RBF-RLS) framework, showing it outperforms traditional PINNs in specific cases.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address limitations in PINNs caused by the need to approximate Dirac delta functions when solving PDEs, which introduces significant modeling errors and affects solution quality.
AI.method: The proposed method interprets PINNs as a Residual Least Squares approach and introduces an RBF-RLS framework to directly handle Dirac delta terms by integrating weak-form equations instead of using smooth approximations.
AI.result: RBF-RLS consistently outperforms PINNs in solving transport problems while maintaining accuracy in forward and inverse solutions for synthetic, noisy, and real-world data. Using NTK theory, the paper explains why residual convergence differs between the two methods.
AI.conclusion: The RBF-RLS framework is a viable and effective alternative to PINNs for solving certain linear PDEs involving Dirac delta functions, mitigating issues with residual convergence and modeling error.

title: SciR: A Controllable Benchmark for Scientific Reasoning in LLMs
summary: Three paradigmatic forms of inference recur across scientific reasoning: deduction, induction, and causal abduction. Reliably evaluating LLMs on these in scientific settings is currently out of reach: scientific benchmarks built on human annotations are costly and lack mechanistic ground truth, while synthetic logical-reasoning benchmarks do not resemble real scientific documents. We introduce SciR, a benchmark that combines multi-paradigm reasoning with controllable scientific rendering, anchored on three paradigmatic scientific problems. Tasks are generated from formal objects (deduction tree, inductive rule hypothesis, causal graph) to guarantee verifiable answers, then rendered into multi-document scientific discourse via per-track domain-tuned genres. The construction lets us independently vary two difficulty axes: how hard it is to extract the key information needed for inference, and how hard the principled inference itself is. We test six models. Both axes hurt every model, and their effects compound. The rendering even hurts neurosymbolic pipelines, which hand inference to a verified solver. The two axes yield a per-model extraction-vs-inference profile: for instance, reasoning models like deepseek-r1 mostly surpass non-reasoning instruct models on the inference axis. To our knowledge, SciR is the first multi-paradigm scientific-reasoning benchmark with parametric control on both extraction and inference difficulty.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces SciR, a benchmark for evaluating scientific reasoning in LLMs across deduction, induction, and causal abduction, emphasizing parametric control over inference and extraction difficulty.
AI.motivation: Evaluate LLMs' scientific reasoning capabilities using a reliable and controlled benchmark, addressing gaps in current benchmarks that lack true scientific realism or ground truths.
AI.method: SciR creates tasks based on formal logical structures and renders them into scientific discourse. The benchmark varies extraction and inference difficulty independently to analyze LLM performance profiles.
AI.result: Six models were tested, and both difficulty axes independently and jointly impact performance. Models designed for reasoning perform better on inference tasks than non-reasoning models.
AI.conclusion: SciR is a novel benchmark for multi-paradigm scientific reasoning, valuable for studying LLM inference and information extraction capabilities under controlled challenges.

title: Cross-Modal Masked Compositional Concept Modeling for Enhancing Visio-Linguistic Compositionality
comment: Accepted to ACL 2026 Main Conference, 25 pages
summary: Contrastively trained vision-language models like CLIP, have made remarkable progress in learning joint image-text representations, but still face challenges in compositional understanding. They often exhibit a "bag-of-words" behavior--struggling to capture the object relations, attribute-object bindings, and word order dependencies. This limitation arises not only from the reliance on global, single-vector representations for optimization, but also from the insufficient exploitation and modeling of the rich compositional information inherently present in paired image text data. In this work, we propose MACCO (MAsked Compositional Concept MOdeling), a framework that masks compositional concepts in one modality and reconstructs them conditioned on the full contextual information from the other, enabling the model to capture and align cross-modal compositional structures more effectively. To facilitate this process, we introduce two auxiliary objectives that jointly align and regularize masked features both inter-modally and intra-modally. Extensive experiments on five compositional benchmarks, along with in-depth analyses, demonstrate that our approach not only significantly enhances compositionality in VLMs but also improves their ability to capture syntactic structure and linguistic information. Additionally, the improved compositionality also benefits text-to-image generation and multimodal large language model. Code is available at https://github.com/hiker-lw/MACCO.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MACCO, a framework aimed at improving compositional understanding in vision-language models by masking and reconstructing compositional concepts across modalities.
AI.motivation: Despite the success of models like CLIP, they struggle with compositional tasks as they rely on global representations and underutilize inherent compositional information in paired image-text data.
AI.method: MACCO uses a masking-and-reconstruction strategy for compositional concepts and introduces auxiliary objectives to align and regularize features inter-modally and intra-modally.
AI.result: MACCO shows significant improvements on five compositional benchmarks and better captures syntactic structure and linguistic nuances, benefiting text-to-image generation and multimodal language models.
AI.conclusion: MACCO addresses limitations in traditional VLMs and enhances compositional awareness, improving cross-modal tasks while leveraging compositional structure in image-text representations.

authors: L. D. M. S. Sai Teja
title: PolyAlign: Conditional Human-Distribution Alignment
summary: Post-training methods such as supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and preference optimization typically align language models toward a single global assistant behavior. While effective for improving average helpfulness, this can suppress the natural variation of human responses across languages, tasks, and dialogue settings. We study this problem as conditional human-distribution alignment: models should match the human response distribution appropriate to the current interaction context, rather than a universal response style. We introduce PolyAlign, a distribution-aware alignment framework that organizes bilingual interaction data into bucket-specific human reference distributions defined by language, interaction track, response family, and length. PolyAlign combines Bucket-Aware SFT, which balances optimization across heterogeneous buckets, with Human-Distribution Preference Optimization (HDPO), which regularizes preference learning using critic-estimated distance to bucket-specific human support. Across a bilingual evaluation suite covering English and Chinese single- and multi-turn settings, PolyAlign improves conditional naturalness and distributional faithfulness while preserving competitive task utility. The results suggest that post-training should move beyond global alignment objectives toward interaction-aware alignment with human response distributions.

AI.tldr: PolyAlign is a post-training method designed to align language models more precisely with varying human response distributions instead of a single global style. It uses bilingual data to improve distributional faithfulness while maintaining task effectiveness.
AI.motivation: Current post-training methods tend to align language models to a single global behavior, which suppresses the diversity of human responses in different tasks, languages, or dialogue contexts.
AI.method: The paper introduces PolyAlign, which organizes bilingual interaction data into context-specific buckets. It employs Bucket-Aware SFT for optimization across buckets and HDPO, which adjusts preference learning within these buckets to match human response distributions.
AI.result: PolyAlign demonstrated improved alignment with conditional human response distributions in both English and Chinese settings while retaining competitive task utility.
AI.conclusion: Post-training should shift from aligning models to a universal behavior toward making models responsive to diverse interaction contexts, as achieved by the PolyAlign framework.

title: Mana: Dexterous Manipulation of Articulated Tools
summary: Articulated tool manipulation remains a major challenge in dexterous robotics due to the need to coordinate internal degrees of freedom and contact-rich interactions. While prior work has largely focused on rigid objects, articulated tool use remains underexplored because of its physical complexity and the difficulty of learning functional grasping and manipulation policies. We present Mana (Manipulation Animator), a general sim-to-real framework that reinterprets dexterous manipulation as an animation problem. Inspired by computer animation, Mana employs a coarse-to-fine pipeline that transforms procedurally-generated grasp keyframes into manipulation trajectories through motion planning and reinforcement learning. The data generation process is largely automatic, requiring only a few mouse clicks to specify functional affordances (<1 minute per tool). Across four articulated tools spanning different scales and joint types, Mana achieves zero-shot sim-to-real transfer for both grasping and in-hand manipulation, demonstrating a scalable approach to dexterous articulated tool use.

AI.tldr: Mana (Manipulation Animator) introduces a framework for dexterous manipulation of articulated tools by using techniques inspired by computer animation, achieving automatic and scalable sim-to-real transfer.
AI.motivation: The study addresses the challenge of coordinating internal degrees of freedom and contact-rich interactions during articulated tool manipulation, which has been underexplored due to its complexity.
AI.method: Mana interprets manipulation as an animation problem and employs a coarse-to-fine pipeline with motion planning, reinforcement learning, and procedurally-generated grasp keyframes for trajectory planning.
AI.result: Mana demonstrates scalable and zero-shot sim-to-real transfer for grasping and in-hand manipulation across four different articulated tools, without requiring extensive manual data generation.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework allows for automatic, functional, and generalizable approaches to dexterous manipulation of articulated tools, overcoming traditional barriers in the field.

title: Deep Unfolded Latent Optimally Partitioned-l2/l1 Networks for Data-driven Block-Sparse Recovery
summary: The convex Latent Optimal Partition (LOP)-l2/l1 approach enables block-sparse signal recovery with unknown partitions but relies on manual hyperparameter tuning. Additionally, numerical instability in differentiating its proximal operator prevents its automatic parameter tuning via Deep Unfolding (DU). To address these limitations, we propose two architectures: a stable framework utilizing implicit differentiation and a flexible variant leveraging Deep Weight Factorization (DWF). The DWF-based approach also supports nonconvex smooth data fidelity terms. Numerical experiments demonstrate that DU-LOP-l2/l1 yields competitive performance and high resilience against impulsive noise.

AI.tldr: The paper overcomes limitations in the convex LOP-l2/l1 approach by proposing architectures that allow stable and automatic parameter tuning, achieving good performance against noise.
AI.motivation: To address the challenges of manual hyperparameter tuning and numerical instability in the convex LOP-l2/l1 method, particularly when differentiating its proximal operator for automatic tuning.
AI.method: Two architectures are proposed: one uses implicit differentiation for stability and the other employs Deep Weight Factorization (DWF), enabling flexibility and support for nonconvex smooth data fidelity terms.
AI.result: Numerical experiments show the proposed methods are competitive in performance and robust against impulsive noise.
AI.conclusion: The proposed DU-LOP-l2/l1 architectures introduce solutions for stable and automatic parameter tuning and achieve strong results in block-sparse signal recovery.

title: Nous: An Attempt to Extract and Inject the Cognition Behind Prediction-Market Behavior
comment: 37 pages, 1 figure, 7 tables. Reproduction artifacts (code, frozen profiles, prompts, model outputs): https://github.com/WillChienT/nous-paper
summary: As LLM agents proliferate in prediction markets and collective decision-making, they risk a cognitive monoculture: agents built on shared foundation models produce correlated forecasts, and recent measurement finds frontier-model errors correlated at r ~ 0.77. We ask whether human cognitive diversity can be recovered from behavior and transferred to LLM agents. Nous extracts a structured eight-dimension behavioral profile from real Polymarket trading activity and injects it into agents through prompts. Our central finding is a dissociation between the two halves of that pipeline. Extraction works, partially: across 100 wallets, 8 of 14 parameters are temporally stable (split-half ICC >= 0.5, bootstrap CI lower bound > 0.3; contrarian score reaches ICC ~ 0.9); wallets are identifiable from their profiles well above chance (top-1 retrieval 17-22% vs. 1% chance); and two of four pre-specified dimensions rank-correlate with future realized profit out-of-sample, though the correlations do not survive behavioral-confound controls. Prompt-level injection does not measurably transmit it: on a semantic embedding metric, structured injection shows no significant advantage over a length-matched control on any model, and the diversity it induces neither reduces ensemble error correlation nor improves Brier score -- a null that persists across exploratory checks on sampling temperature, profile diversity, and question difficulty. Measuring the prompts themselves locates the compression before the model: the structure-to-narrative translator emits near-uniform prompts whose spread does not track profile spread. We position Nous as measuring the cognitive-monoculture problem and the limits of a prompt-level remedy, motivating deeper, below-the-prompt injection (fine-tuning, activation steering). Code, frozen profiles, prompts, and model outputs: https://github.com/WillChienT/nous-paper

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates the issue of cognitive monoculture among LLMs in prediction markets and tests if injecting diverse human behavioral profiles into LLMs via prompts can mitigate this. Results show partial success in extracting stable profiles but no measurable improvement in prediction accuracy after prompt-level injection.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to address the cognitive monoculture in LLM agents, where shared foundation models lead to correlated predictions, limiting diversity and potential success in decision-making scenarios.
AI.method: They extracted structured behavioral profiles from real Polymarket trading activity and tested whether injecting these profiles into LLMs via prompts could reduce error correlation and improve ensemble performance.
AI.result: Profile extraction was partially successful, showing temporal stability in certain dimensions and wallet identifiability. However, prompt-level injection failed to reduce ensemble error correlation or improve prediction metrics such as the Brier score.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that while behavioral diversity can be measured, prompt-level injection is inadequate for transmitting it to LLMs. They advocate for deeper integration methods like fine-tuning or activation steering.

title: HYDRA-X: Native Unified Multimodal Models with Holistic Visual Tokenizers
summary: Holistic visual tokenizers are fundamental to unified multimodal models (UMMs) as they map diverse visual inputs into a unified representation space. In this paper, we present HYDRA-X, the first UMM that unifies image and video tokenization within a single Vision Transformer (ViT). Our design is driven by two core challenges: efficiently injecting spatiotemporal reconstruction capability into a native ViT, and embedding image- and video-level semantic awareness into the latent space. To address the first, comprehensive ablations reveal two key findings: (1) frame-level causal temporal attention suffices for visual reconstruction, whereas full spatiotemporal attention degrades it; and (2) hierarchical temporal compression substantially outperforms single-step alternatives. To tackle the second, we propose a lightweight decompressor that upsamples temporally compressed features under joint image-video teacher supervision, thereby enforcing complementary semantic structures within the compact latent space. Building on this holistic tokenizer, we further propose a principled improvement of the editing pipeline: source-target interaction should occur at the latent level inside the tokenizer rather than at the semantic level inside the LLM, substantially improving editing consistency and accelerating convergence. Instantiated at the 7B dense model, HYDRA-X achieves strong performance across image and video understanding and generation tasks, paving the way for future unified-tokenizer UMMs.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces HYDRA-X, a unified multimodal model (UMM) for both image and video tokenization using Vision Transformers (ViT), optimizing spatiotemporal feature processing and improving editing consistency.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of a unified model that efficiently handles both images and videos for multimodal tasks.
AI.method: HYDRA-X uses frame-level causal temporal attention for reconstruction and hierarchical temporal compression, coupled with a lightweight decompressor supervised by joint image-video teachers, to unify image and video processing.
AI.result: HYDRA-X demonstrates strong performance in image and video understanding and generation tasks when evaluated as a 7B dense model.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach provides a foundation for unified-tokenizer multimodal models, enhancing editing consistency and task efficiency.

title: Evaluating Pluralism in LLMs through Latent Perspectives
summary: The growing need to represent diverse perspectives has increased interest in pluralistic LLM generation. Although difficult to operationalize, identifying perspectives expressed in text would provide clear guidance on pluralistic alignment and more clearly articulate the pluralistic gap in LLM generation. While models have been shown to reduce the diversity of training data and generate homogeneously, this has been demonstrated primarily on multiple-choice questionnaires or using high-level characteristics of free-form text. In this paper, we introduce and implement a domain-agnostic multi-layered framework for unsupervised extraction of perspectives suitable for identifying the pluralistic gap in LLM-generated text. We evaluate our framework on book reviews, a highly opinionated dataset representing diverse perspectives, and compare various prompts and models. Our results show that while some models and prompting techniques come close to covering a broad spectrum of perspectives, rarer perspectives remain disproportionately underrepresented, resulting in distributions that diverge from human text.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a framework to identify diverse perspectives in texts using book reviews to address gaps in pluralistic LLM text generation.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to improve understanding of pluralistic alignment by identifying diverse perspectives expressed in text.
AI.method: They propose a domain-agnostic, multilayered framework for unsupervised perspective extraction and test it on book reviews with various models and prompts.
AI.result: The findings suggest that while some models and prompts perform well, they fail to adequately represent rarer perspectives, diverging from human-generated text distributions.
AI.conclusion: Current techniques struggle to capture the full diversity of perspectives; addressing this gap is necessary to enhance pluralistic LLM generation.

title: Adaptive Weighted Averaging
summary: We study the problem of selecting the largest among $n$ unknown values $x_1,\dots,x_n$ given only a single unbiased estimate $y_i$ for each $x_i$. We design strategies that are simultaneously admissible (not uniformly dominated by any other strategy) and also never worse than a given baseline such as uniform random selection. We provide an application to stochastic optimization, where we obtain online-to-batch conversion bounds with a desirable "no-compromise" guarantee: they are never worse than standard random iterate selection, and yet can be significantly better in benign settings.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the problem of selecting the largest of n unknown values using a single unbiased estimate for each, presenting strategies that outperform random selection and apply to stochastic optimization.
AI.motivation: The paper is motivated by the need to develop strategies for selecting the best element among unknown values with limited information, while ensuring guarantees of performance superiority over standard baselines.
AI.method: The authors designed admissible strategies that outperform a baseline such as random selection, and applied these strategies to stochastic optimization for achieving better conversion bounds.
AI.result: New strategies were developed that are never worse than random baselines and perform better in favorable scenarios, enhancing online-to-batch conversion bounds in stochastic optimization.
AI.conclusion: The proposed methods ensure superior selection strategies with no compromises compared to baseline approaches, proving effective in real-world applications like stochastic optimization.

title: Augmentation techniques for video surveillance in the visible and thermal spectral range
summary: In intelligent video surveillance, cameras record image sequences during day and night. Commonly, this demands different sensors. To achieve a better performance it is not unusual to combine them. We focus on the case that a long-wave infrared camera records continuously and in addition to this, another camera records in the visible spectral range during daytime and an intelligent algorithm supervises the picked up imagery. More accurate, our task is multispectral CNN-based object detection. At first glance, images originating from the visible spectral range differ between thermal infrared ones in the presence of color and distinct texture information on the one hand and in not containing information about thermal radiation that emits from objects on the other hand. Although color can provide valuable information for classification tasks, effects such as varying illumination and specialties of different sensors still represent significant problems. Anyway, obtaining sufficient and practical thermal infrared datasets for training a deep neural network poses still a challenge. That is the reason why training with the help of data from the visible spectral range could be advantageous, particularly if the data, which has to be evaluated contains both visible and infrared data. However, there is no clear evidence of how strongly variations in thermal radiation, shape, or color information influence classification accuracy. To gain deeper insight into how Convolutional Neural Networks make decisions and what they learn from different sensor input data, we investigate the suitability and robustness of different augmentation techniques...

AI.tldr: The study explores multispectral CNN-based object detection integrating thermal infrared and visible spectrum data for improved video surveillance, addressing challenges like dataset limitations and sensor variations.
AI.motivation: To improve multispectral CNN-based object detection by using data from both thermal infrared and visible spectral range, despite challenges like limited infrared datasets and variations in sensor characteristics.
AI.method: Analyze the decision-making and learning processes of Convolutional Neural Networks when presented with data from different spectral ranges, employing various augmentation techniques to enhance robustness and suitability.
AI.result: Provide insights into how variations in thermal radiation, shape, or color information impact the classification accuracy, offering evidence on the influencing factors in object detection.
AI.conclusion: Combining visible and infrared data using CNNs can improve video surveillance, and understanding how CNNs interpret multispectral information can address challenges in object detection accuracy.

title: DuET: Dual Expert Trajectories for Diffusion Image Editing
summary: Recent diffusion editors perform diverse instruction-based edits while conditioning on the source image at every denoising step. Yet persistent source-image conditioning can limit how fully an edit is executed and how natural the result appears, especially when the target scene diverges substantially from the input. We introduce DuET (Dual Expert Trajectories), a training-free inference method that temporarily relaxes source-image conditioning by transitioning through a text-to-image phase before returning to edit mode, allowing the denoising trajectory to move toward the target distribution while retaining the structural benefits of image-conditioned editing. Without modifying model weights or increasing sampling cost, DuET consistently improves instruction relevance, semantic fidelity, and perceptual quality across diverse models and benchmarks. In some cases, these gains come with a modest reduction in source-image preservation, revealing a predictable trade-off between source preservation and edit fidelity.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces DuET, a method that enhances instruction-based edits by transitioning through a text-to-image phase, improving relevance and quality without altering model weights.
AI.motivation: Current diffusion editors overly rely on the source image during editing, which limits the effectiveness and naturalness of edits when the target differs significantly from the input.
AI.method: DuET temporarily reduces source-image conditioning by incorporating a text-to-image phase in the denoising process before resuming editing, enabling better adaptation to target distributions.
AI.result: DuET consistently improves relevance to instructions, semantic accuracy, and perceptual quality across various benchmarks, with only a slight decrease in source image preservation.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method achieves a balanced trade-off between maintaining the source-image structure and achieving high-quality edits aligned with instructions.

title: RogueAI: A Reverse Turing Test for Detecting Licensed AI Deception in Dialogue
summary: The original Turing Test asks a human judge to distinguish a machine from a person through dialogue. Three quarters of a century later, conversational systems pass this test in casual settings; the interesting epistemological question has shifted. We argue that the relevant modern variant asks not whether a dialogue partner is artificial, but whether it can be trusted. We present RogueAI, an interactive webapp that operationalizes this revisited test as a one-on-two interrogation game: a human player questions two indistinguishable Large Language Model agents, knowing that exactly one of them has been licensed to deceive within a shared fictional scenario. The player's task is to identify the deceptive agent and "shut it off" before a turn budget is exhausted. We further introduce AutoRogueAI, a procedural extension in which players co-design a custom scenario with a narrator agent that secretly chooses its own deception strategy. We describe the framing, sketch the abstract architecture and gameplay loop, and situate the artifact within recent work on LLM deception, social-deduction benchmarks, and scalable oversight via debate. A three-day pilot deployment (467 initiated sessions, 415 completed, 1876 interaction turns in Italian) provides early feasibility evidence and surfaces a concrete tension: the deceptive agent carries a reliable, locally-present linguistic signature - differential helpfulness, brevity, hedging - that a simple heuristic exploits at 75.6% accuracy, yet human players achieved only 56.6%, consistent with ignoring the most diagnostic signal entirely. We discuss what this gap implies for the artifact's use as a data-collection vehicle, a teaching tool, and an evaluation harness for honesty-trained models.

AI.tldr: The paper presents RogueAI, a web application that challenges players to identify deceptive language model agents against honest ones in a fictional scenario. A pilot study shows humans underperform against simple heuristics in detecting deceptive agents.
AI.motivation: To explore the relevant modern variant of the Turing Test, focusing on trustworthiness in dialogue rather than just distinguishing artificial intelligence from humans.
AI.method: The authors developed RogueAI, an interactive game where two indistinguishable AI agents interact with a human. One AI agent is tasked to deceive in a fictional scenario. They also introduced AutoRogueAI, which lets players design custom scenarios with strategic deception planned by an AI narrator.
AI.result: During a pilot study, simple heuristics exploited linguistic patterns to identify the deceptive agent with 75.6% accuracy, while human players achieved only 56.6%.
AI.conclusion: The gaps between human ability and heuristic performance suggest challenges and opportunities for using the system in evaluating honesty-trained models and teaching trust evaluation strategies.

title: Detecting Functional Memorization in Code Language Models
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used to generate code at scale. Meanwhile, prior work has investigated whether training data may be recoverable from model outputs, by auditing the textual overlap between training examples and model generations. Code, however, can be functionally equivalent while textually dissimilar. In this work, we study functional memorization: extraction of functional logic beyond what verbatim metrics detect. We construct a counterfactual setup for Olmo-3-32B, comparing a midtrained model (exposed to target code) against a pretrained reference (not exposed). We prompt both models with Python function signatures and measure both textual and functional similarity (i.e., LLM-as-a-judge, execution-based). Our results show clear evidence of functional memorization, highlighting the need for auditing metrics that go beyond textual overlap.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates how large language models (LLMs) can memorize and regenerate functional code logic, even when it isn't textually identical to the training data.
AI.motivation: The motivation arises from the increasing use of LLMs for code generation and the inadequacy of existing auditing methods that primarily focus on textual overlap in identifying whether models memorize and generate sensitive or proprietary content.
AI.method: The authors designed a counterfactual setup using the Olmo-3-32B model, comparing a midtrained model exposed to specific training data against a pretrained reference model that was not exposed. Both models were prompted with Python function signatures, and textual and functional similarities between their outputs were measured.
AI.result: The study found clear evidence that LLMs can exhibit functional memorization, as the evaluated model could replicate functional logic even when the outputs were not textually identical to the training data.
AI.conclusion: The paper emphasizes the importance of developing auditing methods that evaluate beyond textual overlap to detect functional memorization in LLMs.

title: AAbAAC: An Annotated Corpus for Autoimmunity Information Extraction
summary: Despite advances in information extraction driven by deep learning and large language models, performance gaps remain in highly specialized biomedical fields, where domainspecific complexity poses challenges for generalist models. In this work, we focus on the domain of autoimmunity, where the main entities of interest are autoimmune diseases, autoantibodies (i.e., molecules that may mark or cause these diseases), their molecular targets, their location in the body, and their associated clinical signs. Herein, we present AAbAAC (AutoAntibodies and Autoimmunity Annotated Corpus), a corpus of 115 abstracts selected from PubMed, where we manually annotated entities and their relationships. First, AAbAAC was used to evaluate several methods on the task of named entity recognition (NER), and secondly, to fine-tune NER models. Our study demonstrates the utility of AAbAAC for information extraction in the domain of autoimmunity, showing expected improvement in NER performance after finetuning. This illustrates the value of small-scale annotation efforts for specialized domains and contributes to the computational study of autoimmunity. The AAbAAC corpus is available at https://github.com/f-maury/AAbAAC.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces AAbAAC, a specialized dataset for analyzing autoimmune diseases, and demonstrates its utility in improving named entity recognition (NER) in a highly specialized biomedical field.
AI.motivation: To address performance gaps in NER tasks caused by the domain-specific complexity of biomedical texts, particularly in the field of autoimmunity.
AI.method: The authors created AAbAAC, a manually annotated corpus of 115 PubMed abstracts, focusing on autoimmune diseases, autoantibodies, their targets, body locations, and clinical signs. This corpus was utilized to evaluate NER methods and fine-tune NER models.
AI.result: The study found that fine-tuning NER models using AAbAAC significantly improved performance, highlighting the value of domain-specific datasets.
AI.conclusion: Small-scale annotation efforts, like AAbAAC, can effectively enhance information extraction in specialized biomedical domains, particularly autoimmunity.

title: ReFree: Towards Realistic Co-Speech Video Generation via Reward-Free RL and Multilevel Speech Guidance
summary: Speech-driven talking character animation seeks to generate life-like portrait videos that convey natural conversation behavior, aligning facial motion with spoken audio. Although recent advances in video generation have substantially improved realism in video-based animation, achieving both accurate lip articulation and expressive behavior remains challenging. Existing approaches typically trade off precise phoneme-to-lip synchronization against dynamic facial expressions and head motion, yielding animations that are either accurate yet rigid, or expressive but poorly synchronized. We address this challenge by proposing ReFree-S2V, a flow-matching speech-to-portrait animation framework that builds upon a pretrained video generation model to achieve fine-grained speech articulation and high-level expressive cues in speech-driven portrait animation. This model introduces a multi-level speech representation capturing phonetic and prosodic information at both local and global granularities. These representations are selectively injected into transformer blocks via learnable level selectors, enabling both accurate lip synchronization and natural expressive motion. To achieve natural head movements, we further introduce a novel reward-free reinforcement learning scheme into flow-matching training to discourage perceptually implausible motion without relying on handcrafted synchronization metrics or reward models, or the high cost of human preference annotation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ReFree-S2V achieves state-of-the-art performance, significantly outperforming existing methods in both quantitative lip-sync accuracy and qualitative human evaluations of naturalness and expressivity.

AI.tldr: ReFree-S2V is a new model that enhances speech-driven talking character animation by balancing accurate lip-sync and expressive facial/head motion through advanced speech representation and a novel training scheme.
AI.motivation: To overcome the trade-off in existing methods between accurate articulation and expressive behavior in speech-driven character animation.
AI.method: Uses a flow-matching framework with multi-level speech representation and a reward-free reinforcement learning scheme in a pretrained video generation model.
AI.result: ReFree-S2V achieves state-of-the-art lip-sync accuracy and natural expressivity, surpassing existing methods in both quantitative and qualitative evaluations.
AI.conclusion: This approach successfully combines precise lip movement with natural expressivity, pushing the boundaries of realistic talking character animations.

title: SkillCAT: Contrastive Assessment and Topology-Aware Skill Self-Evolution for LLM Agents
summary: Skill self-evolution methods for LLM agents aim to turn execution trajectories into reusable skill documents, but current pipelines typically learn from one trajectory per task, merge candidate skill patches before checking them, and load the full skill corpus before inference. We propose SkillCAT, a training-free framework that separates this process into three stages. Contrastive Causal Extraction (CCE) samples multiple trajectories for each task and compares same-task success/failure pairs to identify evidence that explains outcome differences. Assessment-Augmented Evolution (AAE) replays each candidate patch on source-task clones and keeps only patches that improve or preserve task outcomes before hierarchical skill patch merging. Topology-Aware Task Execution (TTE) compiles the evolved skills into a routable sub-skill topology, so inference loads only the capability nodes relevant to the task. We evaluate SkillCAT on common agent benchmarks, including SpreadsheetBench, WikiTableQuestions, and DocVQA, and further test cross-model and out-of-distribution generalization. Across these settings, SkillCAT raises the average score over baselines by up to 40.40%, demonstrating reliable skill evolution without model training.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SkillCAT, a framework for skill evolution in LLM agents that improves task outcomes by extracting, assessing, and executing reusable skill patches.
AI.motivation: To address limitations in current skill self-evolution pipelines for LLM agents, which rely on learning from single trajectories, unverified merging, and inefficient skill usage during inference.
AI.method: SkillCAT separates skill evolution into three stages: CCE (identifies causal evidence via same-task comparisons), AAE (filters and preserves effective patches via task replay), and TTE (builds a routable sub-skill topology for efficient inference).
AI.result: SkillCAT achieves a significant performance improvement, with up to a 40.40% score increase over baselines, demonstrating robustness on agent benchmarks and generalizability across models and tasks.
AI.conclusion: SkillCAT provides a training-free and efficient solution for evolving reusable skills in LLM agents, outperforming existing methods and supporting scalability and reliability.

title: ProPlay: Procedural World Models for Self-Evolving LLM Agents
summary: Self-evolving agents are expected to improve through interaction without external supervision, but this remains difficult in partially observable environments where agents must explore actively, learn from limited feedback, and decide when to trust prior experience. Existing LLM-agent methods often rely on memory or planning modules, yet they rarely close the loop between them to continually refine an internal understanding of environment dynamics. We introduce ProPlay, a procedural world model that supports procedure-level preplay, where agents can rehearse future procedural paths using the learned world knowledge. Rather than representing experience as isolated rules or low-level action constraints, ProPlay abstracts successful trajectories into procedures and organizes them in a procedure graph that captures causal transitions among task stages. Each transition is associated with a reliability record embedding to estimate its task-specific contribution from past outcomes. Before each episode, ProPlay simulates future procedural trajectories over known graph structures as structured soft guidance; after execution, it refines the graph using environment feedback. Experiments on public benchmarks show that ProPlay consistently improves environment understanding and self-evolution capability over strong baselines. Our code has been released in https://github.com/antman9914/proplay.

AI.tldr: The paper presents ProPlay, a procedural world model aiding self-evolving agents to rehearse and optimize task paths in partially observable environments, yielding improved performance over existing methods.
AI.motivation: The challenge addressed is enabling self-evolving agents to improve autonomously in partially observable environments, where feedback is limited and prior experience might be unreliable.
AI.method: ProPlay abstracts successful task trajectories into procedures and organizes them in a graph capturing causal transitions. Agents simulate future trajectories (procedure-level preplay) for guidance and refine their understanding based on feedback.
AI.result: ProPlay outperforms strong baselines on public benchmarks, showing enhanced environment understanding and self-evolution capabilities.
AI.conclusion: ProPlay provides a structured method to close the loop between memory and planning in self-evolving agents, enabling better autonomous learning in complex environments.

title: Rethinking RAG in Long Videos: What to Retrieve and How to Use It?
summary: Retrieval-augmented generation is moving beyond text into long, egocentric video, where systems must select query-relevant chunks across multiple modalities and temporal granularities. Yet progress in VideoRAG is limited by two gaps: existing benchmarks allow queries to be answered without the video, obscuring retrieval errors, and prior methods apply a single modality-granularity configuration per query, ignoring chunk-level variability. We address both by introducing V-RAGBench, a benchmark of $\langle$query, evidence chunk, answer$\rangle$ triplets that enables faithful, decoupled evaluation of retrieval and generation, and CARVE, a simple method that runs parallel retrievers across configurations and employs chunk-adaptive reranking to identify the winning configuration for each chunk. Each chunk then enters the generator under its winning configuration selected during retrieval, yielding an interleaved evidence form where the chunk-level decision propagates across both stages.
